topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Random Variable

A **Random Variable (RV)** is a real-valued function defined on the sample space of a random experiment. Mathematically, it is a mapping:

$$X : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

where every outcome $\omega \in \mathcal{S}$ of the sample space $\mathcal{S}$ is assigned a unique real number $X(\omega)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A random variable is a *function* (not a variable in the algebraic sense) whose value is a real number determined by the outcome of a random experiment. The set of all possible values it can take is called the **Range** or **Image** of the random variable.

> [!NOTE]
> A random variable is **always** denoted by an uppercase letter (e.g., $X$, $Y$, $Z$), while its observed value is denoted by a lowercase letter (e.g., $x$, $y$, $z$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are tossing **two fair coins** simultaneously. The sample space is:

$$\mathcal{S} = \{HH, HT, TH, TT\}$$

Now, suppose we *define a rule*: "Let $X$ be the **number of Heads** that appear." Then:

- $X(HH) = 2$
- $X(HT) = 1$
- $X(TH) = 1$
- $X(TT) = 0$

So $X$ converts each random outcome into a measurable real number. This rule — the assignment of a real number to each outcome — **is the random variable**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Machine Learning):** In a spam detection system, the *email* is the random outcome. The "length of the email in words" or "the count of suspicious keywords" can each be a random variable. The randomness comes from the unknown incoming email; the variable measures a property of it.

## 1.3 Classification of Random Variables

Random variables are broadly classified into two main types based on the **nature of values** they can take:

| Type | Nature of Range | Examples | Notation |
|------|-----------------|----------|----------|
| **Discrete Random Variable (DRV)** | Countable (finite or countably infinite) | Number of defective items, count of emails received, dice roll result | $X \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ |
| **Continuous Random Variable (CRV)** | Uncountable (an interval of real numbers) | Height of students, time to complete a task, temperature | $X \in [a, b]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The classification is essential because *discrete* and *continuous* random variables use **different mathematical tools** to describe their probabilities (PMF vs. PDF).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Quick-Glossary

- **Sample Space ($\mathcal{S}$):** Set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment.
- **Range / Image of $X$:** The set $\{x \in \mathbb{R} : X(\omega) = x \text{ for some } \omega \in \mathcal{S}\}$.
- **Event $\{X \in A\}$:** The set of all outcomes $\omega$ for which $X(\omega) \in A$, where $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$.
- **Realization:** The actual value observed during a single trial of the experiment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Probability Mass Function (PMF) — For Discrete Random Variables

For a **Discrete Random Variable** $X$ taking values $x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots$, the **Probability Mass Function** $P_X(x)$ is defined as:

$$P_X(x_i) = P(X = x_i) = p_i$$

### Properties of a Valid PMF

For a valid PMF, the following two conditions **must** be satisfied:

- **Non-negativity:** $P_X(x_i) \geq 0$ for every $i$.
- **Unity of total probability:** $\sum_{i} P_X(x_i) = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A common mistake is forgetting the *non-negativity* condition. Always state both conditions explicitly in definitions for full marks.

## 2.2 Probability Density Function (PDF) — For Continuous Random Variables

For a **Continuous Random Variable** $X$, probability is assigned to *intervals*, not individual points. The function $f_X(x)$ is called the **Probability Density Function** if:

$$P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f_X(x)\, dx$$

### Properties of a Valid PDF

- **Non-negativity:** $f_X(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Unity of total probability:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$.
- **Point probability is zero:** $P(X = c) = 0$ for any single point $c$.

> [!WARNING]
> A PDF value $f_X(c)$ is **not** a probability itself; it is a probability *density*. The actual probability is obtained only by integration over an interval.

## 2.3 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) — Universal

The **Cumulative Distribution Function** $F_X(x)$ is the single most important function for *any* random variable (discrete or continuous). It is defined as:

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$$

### Properties of the CDF

The CDF $F_X(x)$ must satisfy the following **KTU high-yield** properties:

- **Monotonically non-decreasing:** $F_X(x_1) \leq F_X(x_2)$ whenever $x_1 \leq x_2$.
- **Limit behavior:** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$ and $\lim_{x \to \infty} F_X(x) = 1$.
- **Right-continuity:** $F_X(x^+) = F_X(x)$.
- **Probability from CDF:** $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$.

### CDF Formulas — Discrete and Continuous

For a **discrete** random variable:

$$F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} P_X(x_i)$$

For a **continuous** random variable:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$$

And the reverse relationship:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$$

## 2.4 Expectation (Mean) and Variance

### Mathematical Expectation $E[X]$

For **discrete** $X$:

$$E[X] = \mu = \sum_{i} x_i \, P_X(x_i)$$

For **continuous** $X$:

$$E[X] = \mu = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_X(x)\, dx$$

### Variance $\text{Var}(X)$ and Standard Deviation

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$$

The standard deviation is the positive square root:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Discrete Formula | Continuous Formula | Range |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|-------|
| **PMF / PDF** | $P_X(x_i) = p_i$ | $f_X(x) \geq 0$ | $x \in S_X$ |
| **Validity** | $\sum p_i = 1$ | $\int f_X(x)\, dx = 1$ | over $S_X$ |
| **CDF** | $F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p_i$ | $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| **Reverse relation** | Step function from PMF | $f_X(x) = \dfrac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$ | – |
| **Mean** | $E[X] = \sum x_i p_i$ | $E[X] = \int x\, f_X(x)\, dx$ | – |
| **Variance** | $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - \mu^2$ | $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - \mu^2$ | – |
| **Probability over interval** | $\sum_{a < x_i \leq b} p_i$ | $\int_{a}^{b} f_X(x)\, dx$ | – |
| **Moment of order $k$** | $E[X^k] = \sum x_i^k\, p_i$ | $E[X^k] = \int x^k f_X(x)\, dx$ | – |
| **Median** | $F_X(m) \geq 0.5$, $F_X(m^-) \leq 0.5$ | $F_X(m) = 0.5$ | – |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In Computer Science, random variables are used to model *network latency* (continuous), *packet arrival counts* (discrete, Poisson), and *algorithmic running times* (often continuous, exponential/normal distributions). The CDF is especially critical in reliability engineering to compute $P(\text{System fails by time } T)$.

## 2.6 Functions of a Random Variable

If $Y = g(X)$ is a function of a random variable $X$, then:

For **discrete** $X$:

$$E[Y] = E[g(X)] = \sum_{i} g(x_i)\, P_X(x_i)$$

For **continuous** $X$:

$$E[Y] = E[g(X)] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x)\, f_X(x)\, dx$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Identity (Law of the Unconscious Statistician — LOTUS):** Even if we do not know the distribution of $Y$, we can compute $E[g(X)]$ using the distribution of $X$ directly via the above formulas.

## 2.7 Important Theoretical Properties

- **Linearity of Expectation:** $E[aX + b] = aE[X] + b$ for any constants $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Variance Transform:** $\text{Var}(aX + b) = a^2\, \text{Var}(X)$.
- **For independent** $X, Y$: $E[XY] = E[X]\, E[Y]$ and $\text{Var}(X+Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$.
- **Chebyshev's Inequality:** $P(\vert X - \mu \vert \geq k\sigma) \leq \dfrac{1}{k^2}$ for $k > 0$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From PMF to CDF (Discrete Case)

**Problem:** Given the PMF of a discrete random variable $X$ that takes values $\{1, 2, 4, 8\}$ with probabilities $\{0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4\}$. Derive the CDF.

**Step 1:** Recall the definition. The CDF for a discrete variable is the cumulative sum of PMF values up to $x$:

$$F_X(x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} P_X(x_i)$$

**Step 2:** For values $x < 1$, no values of $X$ satisfy $x_i \leq x$. Therefore:

$$F_X(x) = 0, \quad x < 1$$

**Step 3:** For $1 \leq x < 2$, only the value $x_1 = 1$ is included:

$$F_X(x) = P_X(1) = 0.1$$

**Step 4:** For $2 \leq x < 4$, we include both $x_1 = 1$ and $x_2 = 2$:

$$F_X(x) = P_X(1) + P_X(2) = 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.3$$

**Step 5:** For $4 \leq x < 8$, we include $x_1, x_2, x_3$:

$$F_X(x) = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.6$$

**Step 6:** For $x \geq 8$, all values are included:

$$F_X(x) = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.4 = 1.0$$

**Step 7:** Verify total probability sums to 1 (a necessary condition):

$$\sum_{i} P_X(x_i) = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.4 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Resulting CDF:**

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 1 \\ 0.1, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ 0.3, & 2 \leq x < 4 \\ 0.6, & 4 \leq x < 8 \\ 1.0, & x \geq 8 \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The CDF of a discrete random variable is a **right-continuous step function** with jumps at the points where the random variable has nonzero probability.

## 3.2 Derivation: From PDF to CDF (Continuous Case)

**Problem:** Given a continuous random variable $X$ with PDF:

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} 2x, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Derive the CDF $F_X(x)$.

**Step 1:** The general relation between CDF and PDF is:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2:** For $x < 0$, the integration range is entirely outside the support of $f_X$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} 0\, dt = 0$$

**Step 3:** For $0 \leq x \leq 1$, integrate from $0$ to $x$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{x} 2t\, dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral using the power rule $\int t^n\, dt = \dfrac{t^{n+1}}{n+1}$:

$$F_X(x) = \left[ t^2 \right]_{0}^{x} = x^2 - 0^2 = x^2$$

**Step 5:** For $x > 1$, the entire support $[0, 1]$ is contained in the integration:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{1} 2t\, dt = \left[ t^2 \right]_{0}^{1} = 1$$

**Step 6:** Sanity check — verify $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{1} 2x\, dx = \left[ x^2 \right]_{0}^{1} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Resulting CDF:**

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ x^2, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 1, & x > 1 \end{cases}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Mean and Variance (Worked Analytical Example)

**Problem:** For the same PDF $f_X(x) = 2x$ on $[0,1]$, compute $E[X]$ and $\text{Var}(X)$.

### Computing $E[X]$

**Step 1:** Apply the continuous expectation formula:

$$E[X] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x\, f_X(x)\, dx$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the PDF and limits (since $f_X = 0$ outside $[0,1]$):

$$E[X] = \int_{0}^{1} x \cdot 2x\, dx = \int_{0}^{1} 2x^2\, dx$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$E[X] = \left[ \frac{2x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{2}{3} - 0 = \frac{2}{3}$$

### Computing $E[X^2]$

**Step 4:** Apply the $k$-th moment formula with $k = 2$:

$$E[X^2] = \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \cdot 2x\, dx = \int_{0}^{1} 2x^3\, dx$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate:

$$E[X^2] = \left[ \frac{2x^4}{4} \right]_{0}^{1} = \left[ \frac{x^4}{2} \right]_{0}^{1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

### Computing $\text{Var}(X)$

**Step 6:** Use the identity $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{2} - \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{4}{9}$$

**Step 7:** Find a common denominator (18) and subtract:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \frac{9}{18} - \frac{8}{18} = \frac{1}{18}$$

### Summary of Result

$$E[X] = \frac{2}{3}, \qquad \text{Var}(X) = \frac{1}{18}, \qquad \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{18}} = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Median of a Continuous Distribution

**Problem:** Find the median $m$ of $X$ with PDF $f_X(x) = 2x$ on $[0,1]$.

**Step 1:** By definition, the median satisfies:

$$F_X(m) = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** From §3.2, $F_X(x) = x^2$ for $x \in [0,1]$. So:

$$m^2 = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $m$:

$$m = \sqrt{0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: 50% of the time, $X$ falls below $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Standard Distributions

The following table summarizes standard distributions that a KTU student **must know** from Module 1:

| Distribution | Type | PMF / PDF | Mean | Variance | Range |
|--------------|------|-----------|------|----------|-------|
| **Bernoulli$(p)$** | Discrete | $P(X=1) = p$, $P(X=0) = 1-p$ | $p$ | $p(1-p)$ | $\{0,1\}$ |
| **Binomial$(n,p)$** | Discrete | $P(X=k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ | $np$ | $np(1-p)$ | $\{0, 1, \ldots, n\}$ |
| **Poisson$(\lambda)$** | Discrete | $P(X=k) = \dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k}{k!}$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ |
| **Uniform$(a,b)$** | Continuous | $f_X(x) = \dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | $\dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12}$ | $[a,b]$ |
| **Exponential$(\lambda)$** | Continuous | $f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | $[0,\infty)$ |
| **Normal$(\mu, \sigma^2)$** | Continuous | $f_X(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** In semester exams, you will typically be asked to derive the mean and variance of one of these standard distributions using the *definition* (not by just quoting the formula). Practice the integration steps carefully.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Random Variables

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **hierarchical classification** of random variables, their associated probability functions, and the descriptive statistics derived from them:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Space S] --> B[Random Variable X]
    B --> C{Type of X}
    C -->|Countable Range| D[Discrete Random Variable]
    C -->|Uncountable Range| E[Continuous Random Variable]
    D --> D1[Probability Mass Function PMF]
    D --> D2[Cumulative Distribution Function CDF]
    D --> D3[Mean, Variance, Moments]
    E --> E1[Probability Density Function PDF]
    E --> E2[Cumulative Distribution Function CDF]
    E --> E3[Mean, Variance, Moments]
    D1 --> D2
    E1 --> E2
    D2 --> D3
    E2 --> E3
    D --> F1[Examples: Dice, Defects, Counts]
    E --> F2[Examples: Height, Time, Temperature]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — From Experiment to Inference

The following block diagram maps out the *engineering pipeline* used in real-world applications (e.g., in data science, queueing theory, and ML) to convert a random experiment into actionable probabilistic inference.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Random Experiment] --> step2[Sample Space S]
    step2 --> step3[Define Random Variable X]
    step3 --> step4{Discrete or Continuous}
    step4 -->|Discrete| step5[PMF P_X x]
    step4 -->|Continuous| step6[PDF f_X x]
    step5 --> step7[Compute CDF F_X x]
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8[Derive Mean and Variance]
    step8 --> step9[Apply Inferential Tools]
    step9 --> stepA[Engineering Decisions]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Relationships Between PMF, PDF, and CDF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pmfA[PMF P_X k] -->|Cumulative Sum| cdfB[CDF F_X x]
    pdfA[PDF f_X x] -->|Integration| cdfB
    cdfB -->|Differentiation| pdfA
    cdfB -->|Step Function Jump| pmfA
    pmfA --> meanA[Mean E X]
    pdfA --> meanA
    pmfA --> varA[Variance Var X]
    pdfA --> varA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The **CDF** is the *universal bridge* — it works for both discrete and continuous RVs.
> - The **PMF and PDF** are mutually exclusive (one is for discrete, the other for continuous RVs).
> - **Mean and Variance** are computed from whichever of the three functions is most convenient.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define a *random variable*. Differentiate between a discrete and a continuous random variable with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

A *random variable* $X$ is a real-valued function defined on the sample space $\mathcal{S}$ of a random experiment, that is, $X : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, which assigns a unique real number to every outcome.

| Aspect | Discrete Random Variable | Continuous Random Variable |
|--------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Nature of values | Countable (finite or countably infinite) | Uncountable (intervals) |
| Probability description | Probability Mass Function (PMF) | Probability Density Function (PDF) |
| Point probability | $P(X = x_i) > 0$ possible | $P(X = c) = 0$ for any single point |
| Example | Number of heads in 3 coin tosses | Time taken to download a file |

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** State and explain the properties of the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) $F_X(x)$.

**Model Answer:**

The CDF $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ has the following four properties:

1. **Monotonically non-decreasing:** If $x_1 \leq x_2$, then $F_X(x_1) \leq F_X(x_2)$.
2. **Limit at $-\infty$:** $\lim_{x \to -\infty} F_X(x) = 0$.
3. **Limit at $+\infty$:** $\lim_{x \to \infty} F_X(x) = 1$.
4. **Right-continuity:** $F_X(x^+) = F_X(x)$ for all $x$.

Additionally, the probability of any interval is obtained as:
$$P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$$

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** The probability mass function of a discrete random variable $X$ is given by:
> $$P_X(x) = \begin{cases} k\, x, & x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
> Find the value of $k$, the cumulative distribution function $F_X(x)$, and compute $E[X]$ and $\text{Var}(X)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Find $k$ using the total probability condition.**

Since $\sum P_X(x) = 1$:

$$\sum_{x=1}^{5} kx = k(1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5) = 15k = 1$$

Therefore:

$$k = \frac{1}{15}$$

**Step 2: Construct the PMF table.**

| $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_X(x)$ | $\frac{1}{15}$ | $\frac{2}{15}$ | $\frac{3}{15}$ | $\frac{4}{15}$ | $\frac{5}{15}$ |

**Step 3: Compute $E[X]$.**

$$E[X] = \sum_{x=1}^{5} x \cdot P_X(x) = \sum_{x=1}^{5} x \cdot \frac{x}{15} = \frac{1}{15} \sum_{x=1}^{5} x^2$$

Using $\sum_{x=1}^{n} x^2 = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}$ with $n = 5$:

$$\sum_{x=1}^{5} x^2 = \frac{5 \cdot 6 \cdot 11}{6} = 55$$

Therefore:

$$E[X] = \frac{55}{15} = \frac{11}{3} \approx 3.667$$

**Step 4: Compute $E[X^2]$.**

$$E[X^2] = \sum_{x=1}^{5} x^2 \cdot P_X(x) = \sum_{x=1}^{5} x^2 \cdot \frac{x}{15} = \frac{1}{15} \sum_{x=1}^{5} x^3$$

Using $\sum_{x=1}^{n} x^3 = \left[\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}\right]^2$ with $n = 5$:

$$\sum_{x=1}^{5} x^3 = \left(\frac{5 \cdot 6}{2}\right)^2 = 15^2 = 225$$

Therefore:

$$E[X^2] = \frac{225}{15} = 15$$

**Step 5: Compute the variance.**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = 15 - \left(\frac{11}{3}\right)^2 = 15 - \frac{121}{9}$$

Converting to a common denominator:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \frac{135}{9} - \frac{121}{9} = \frac{14}{9} \approx 1.556$$

**Step 6: Construct the CDF $F_X(x)$.**

The CDF is the cumulative sum:

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 1 \\ \frac{1}{15}, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ \frac{3}{15}, & 2 \leq x < 3 \\ \frac{6}{15}, & 3 \leq x < 4 \\ \frac{10}{15}, & 4 \leq x < 5 \\ 1, & x \geq 5 \end{cases}$$

> **(b)** A continuous random variable $X$ has PDF:
> $$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} kx^2, & 0 \leq x \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
> Find $k$, the CDF $F_X(x)$, and $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Find $k$ using the total probability condition.**

$$\int_{0}^{3} kx^2\, dx = 1$$

Evaluating:

$$k \cdot \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{3} = k \cdot \frac{27}{3} = 9k = 1$$

Therefore:

$$k = \frac{1}{9}$$

**Step 2: Construct the CDF.**

For $x < 0$:

$$F_X(x) = 0$$

For $0 \leq x \leq 3$:

$$F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{t^2}{9}\, dt = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \left[ \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{x} = \frac{x^3}{27}$$

For $x > 3$:

$$F_X(x) = 1$$

Therefore:

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \dfrac{x^3}{27}, & 0 \leq x \leq 3 \\ 1, & x > 3 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$.**

Method 1: Direct integration using PDF.

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{x^2}{9}\, dx = \frac{1}{9} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{1}^{2} = \frac{1}{27} (8 - 1) = \frac{7}{27}$$

Method 2: Using the CDF.

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = F_X(2) - F_X(1) = \frac{8}{27} - \frac{1}{27} = \frac{7}{27} \approx 0.2593$$

**Final Answer:** $k = \frac{1}{9}$, $F_X(x)$ as above, $P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = \frac{7}{27}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** The CDF of a random variable $X$ is given by:
> $$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \frac{x^2}{4}, & 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 1, & x > 2 \end{cases}$$
> Find the PDF, the mean $E[X]$, and the variance $\text{Var}(X)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Determine the type of RV.**

Since $F_X(x)$ is continuous, $X$ is a continuous random variable.

**Step 2: Compute the PDF by differentiating the CDF.**

For $0 \leq x \leq 2$:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} \left( \frac{x^2}{4} \right) = \frac{x}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{x}{2}, & 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Verify the validity of the PDF.**

Check non-negativity: $f_X(x) = \frac{x}{2} \geq 0$ for $x \in [0, 2]$. ✓

Check total probability:

$$\int_{0}^{2} \frac{x}{2}\, dx = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Compute $E[X]$.**

$$E[X] = \int_{0}^{2} x \cdot \frac{x}{2}\, dx = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{x^2}{2}\, dx = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{8}{3} = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Step 5: Compute $E[X^2]$.**

$$E[X^2] = \int_{0}^{2} x^2 \cdot \frac{x}{2}\, dx = \int_{0}^{2} \frac{x^3}{2}\, dx = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^4}{4} \right]_{0}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4 = 2$$

**Step 6: Compute the variance.**

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = 2 - \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 = 2 - \frac{16}{9} = \frac{18 - 16}{9} = \frac{2}{9}$$

**Final Answer:** $f_X(x) = \frac{x}{2}$ on $[0,2]$; $E[X] = \frac{4}{3}$; $\text{Var}(X) = \frac{2}{9}$.

---

> **(b)** If $X$ is uniformly distributed on $[0, 4]$, find the PDF, CDF, and $P(1 < X < 3)$. Also find the mean and variance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Write the PDF of Uniform$(0, 4)$.**

For a continuous uniform distribution on $[a, b] = [0, 4]$:

$$f_X(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{b - a} = \dfrac{1}{4}, & 0 \leq x \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Compute the CDF.**

$$F_X(x) = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{1}{4}\, dt = \frac{x}{4}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq 4$$

Therefore:

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ \dfrac{x}{4}, & 0 \leq x \leq 4 \\ 1, & x > 4 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Compute $P(1 < X < 3)$.**

Method 1: Using the PDF.

$$P(1 < X < 3) = \int_{1}^{3} \frac{1}{4}\, dx = \frac{1}{4} \cdot (3 - 1) = \frac{1}{2}$$

Method 2: Using the CDF.

$$P(1 < X < 3) = F_X(3) - F_X(1) = \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4: Compute the mean.**

For uniform on $[a, b]$:

$$E[X] = \frac{a + b}{2} = \frac{0 + 4}{2} = 2$$

**Verification via integration:**

$$E[X] = \int_{0}^{4} x \cdot \frac{1}{4}\, dx = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{4} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 8 = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Compute the variance.**

For uniform on $[a, b]$:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \frac{(b - a)^2}{12} = \frac{(4 - 0)^2}{12} = \frac{16}{12} = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Verification via integration:**

$$E[X^2] = \int_{0}^{4} x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{4}\, dx = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{4} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{64}{3} = \frac{16}{3}$$

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = \frac{16}{3} - 4 = \frac{16 - 12}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $f_X(x) = \frac{1}{4}$, $F_X(x) = \frac{x}{4}$, $P(1 < X < 3) = \frac{1}{2}$, $E[X] = 2$, $\text{Var}(X) = \frac{4}{3}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Random Variable Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to find the normalizing constant $k$ FIRST.** Many students plug in $k = 1$ and proceed. Always start by enforcing $\sum P_X(x) = 1$ (discrete) or $\int f_X(x)\, dx = 1$ (continuous) to find $k$. This is worth **2–3 marks** standalone.
>
> 2. **Confusing the CDF with the PDF.** A common error is writing $f_X(x)$ for $F_X(x)$ or vice versa. The CDF is *cumulative*; the PDF is a *density*.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the indicator condition for validity.** A PMF must satisfy both $P_X(x_i) \geq 0$ AND $\sum P_X(x_i) = 1$. Forgetting either one loses marks.
>
> 4. **Computing $\text{Var}(X)$ using $E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ incorrectly.** The correct identity is $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - \mu^2$, where $\mu = E[X]$. Do **not** write $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - E[X]$ — this is a very common algebraic error.
>
> 5. **CDF must be right-continuous.** For a discrete RV, the CDF has a *jump* (not a slope) at each value of $X$. The interval notation $a \leq x < b$ (closed on the left, open on the right) is critical to maintain right-continuity.
>
> 6. **For continuous RVs, do not write $P(X = c)$.** Always state that $P(X = c) = 0$ for any single point. Writing a nonzero value for $P(X = c)$ will be marked wrong.
>
> 7. **Numerical accuracy.** In integration problems, show the antiderivative step clearly. Skipping from $\int x^2\, dx$ directly to $\frac{x^3}{3}$ is acceptable, but jumping to the final numerical value without intermediate steps often attracts partial deductions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Random Variables**

- **Random Variable:** A function $X : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that maps outcomes to real numbers. **Not** a random quantity in the colloquial sense — it is a deterministic function of a random input.
- **Discrete vs Continuous:** Use **PMF** for discrete (summation), use **PDF** for continuous (integration).
- **CDF Properties to Memorize:** Non-decreasing, $F_X(-\infty) = 0$, $F_X(+\infty) = 1$, right-continuous, $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$.
- **PMF Validity:** $P_X(x) \geq 0$ and $\sum P_X(x) = 1$.
- **PDF Validity:** $f_X(x) \geq 0$ and $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x)\, dx = 1$.
- **Point Probability for Continuous:** $P(X = c) = 0$.
- **Mean Formula:** $E[X] = \sum x_i p_i$ (discrete) or $E[X] = \int x f_X(x)\, dx$ (continuous).
- **Variance Formula:** $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ — *always* use this identity.
- **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)} \geq 0$.
- **Linear Transforms:** $E[aX + b] = aE[X] + b$ and $\text{Var}(aX + b) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$.
- **CDF–PDF Relation:** $F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(t)\, dt$ and $f_X(x) = \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x)$.
- **Standard Distributions to Know Cold (Mean & Variance Formulas):** Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Uniform, Exponential, Normal.
- **Median:** The value $m$ such that $F_X(m) = 0.5$ (for continuous); for discrete, $F_X(m) \geq 0.5$ and $F_X(m^-) \leq 0.5$.
- **Engineering Connection:** Random variables model everything from network latency (Exponential) to ML feature noise (Normal) to defect counts in a batch (Binomial/Poisson).
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** A PDF $f_X(c)$ is **not** a probability — it is a *density* (units: probability per unit $x$). Only $\int f_X(x)\, dx$ over an interval gives a probability.
- **Order of Operations in a New Problem:** (1) Find the normalizing constant $k$, (2) verify the validity conditions, (3) compute the CDF if required, (4) compute $E[X]$ and $E[X^2]$, (5) derive $\text{Var}(X)$ and $\sigma$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Random Variables and Their Probability Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT301 - MATHEMATICS FOR COMPUTER AND INFORMATION SCIENCE-3
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete Random Variables and Their Probability Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Random Variable — The Formal Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Random Variable — KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A **random variable** is a real-valued function defined on the sample space $S$ of a random experiment. Formally, it is a function
> $$X : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$
> that assigns a unique real number $X(\omega)$ to every sample point $\omega \in S$.

In simpler words, a random variable is **not random and not a variable in the algebraic sense** — it is a **rule** that converts outcomes of a probabilistic experiment into numbers that we can perform mathematical operations on.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Random Variables (KTU Taxonomy)**
> - **Discrete Random Variable:** Takes a **finite** or **countably infinite** set of values (e.g., $0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$).
> - **Continuous Random Variable:** Takes all values in an **interval** (uncountably infinite) — covered in a later module.

## 1.2 Discrete Random Variable — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Discrete Random Variable)**
> A random variable $X$ is called **discrete** if it can take only a **finite** or **countably infinite** number of distinct values. The set of possible values (the **range** of $X$) is denoted by $R_X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots\}$.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Numbered Marbles"
Imagine a box containing marbles labeled $0$, $1$, $2$, $3$, and $4$ (no fractional labels, no decimal labels). You pick a marble blindfolded, and the **label on the marble** becomes the value of your random variable $X$. Since you can ONLY get whole-number labels from this box, $X$ is discrete. The probability of each label is determined by how many marbles of that label are in the box. This entire mapping — labels to probabilities — is what we call a **probability distribution**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Context (Computer & Information Science)**
> In CS, discrete random variables model:
> - The **number of bits** received in error over a channel per second.
> - The **count of packets** arriving at a router in a fixed time window.
> - The **number of server requests** hitting a web server in a minute.
> - The **outcome of a coin flip** (Bernoulli trial) in randomized algorithms.

## 1.3 Probability Distribution of a Discrete Random Variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Probability Mass Function — PMF)**
> For a discrete random variable $X$ with range $R_X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots\}$, the **Probability Mass Function** $p_X(x)$ assigns to each possible value $x_i$ the probability that $X$ equals $x_i$:
> $$p_X(x_i) = P(X = x_i)$$

The collection $\{p_X(x_i)\}_{i=1}^{\infty}$ together with the range $R_X$ constitutes the **probability distribution** of the discrete random variable $X$.

### Axiomatic Properties of a Valid PMF

For any PMF $p_X(x)$, **two fundamental conditions** must be satisfied:

$$p_X(x_i) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} p_X(x_i) = 1$$

The first axiom ensures **non-negativity** (probabilities cannot be negative), and the second axiom ensures the **total probability** of all mutually exclusive outcomes sums to **1** (certainty).

## 1.4 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) — The Bridge

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (CDF for Discrete Random Variable)**
> The **Cumulative Distribution Function** $F_X(x)$ of a discrete random variable $X$ is defined as:
> $$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p_X(x_i)$$
> for every real number $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

The CDF is always a **right-continuous**, **non-decreasing** function with the boundary limits $F_X(-\infty) = 0$ and $F_X(+\infty) = 1$.

### Visualizing the CDF — The "Summing Up" View

The CDF at any point $x$ gives the **total probability accumulated** from the smallest value of $X$ up to and including $x$. For a discrete $X$ with values $\{1, 2, 3\}$ and PMF values $p(1), p(2), p(3)$:

$$F_X(0) = 0, \quad F_X(1) = p(1), \quad F_X(2) = p(1) + p(2), \quad F_X(3) = 1$$

This is a **step function** that jumps at each value of $X$ by an amount equal to the PMF at that point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Six Pillars of Discrete Distributions

### Pillar 1: Probability Mass Function (PMF) — Already Established
The PMF is the **atomic building block** of a discrete distribution. It answers: "What is the probability that $X$ takes the specific value $x_i$?"

### Pillar 2: Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)
The CDF **aggregates** the PMF from the left. It answers: "What is the probability that $X$ is at most $x$?"

**Why does the CDF matter in KTU exams?**
- It is the **bridge** between discrete and continuous worlds.
- It allows computing interval probabilities: $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$.
- It is **uniquely defined** even when the PMF has discontinuities (jump points).

### Pillar 3: Expected Value (Mean) — The "Center of Mass"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Expected Value / Mathematical Expectation)**
> For a discrete random variable $X$ with PMF $p_X(x_i)$:
> $$E[X] = \mu = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot p_X(x_i)$$
> provided the sum converges absolutely; otherwise, $E[X]$ is said to **not exist**.

**Intuition:** The expected value is the **weighted average** of all possible values of $X$, where the weights are the probabilities. It is the **long-run average** of $X$ if the experiment is repeated infinitely many times.

### Pillar 4: Variance and Standard Deviation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Variance)**
> The variance of a discrete random variable $X$ measures the **average squared deviation** from the mean:
> $$\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2 = E[(X - \mu)^2] = \sum_{i} (x_i - \mu)^2 \cdot p_X(x_i)$$

**Computational Shortcut Formula (Highly Useful for KTU Problems):**
$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$$
where $E[X^2] = \sum_{i} x_i^2 \cdot p_X(x_i)$.

The **standard deviation** is $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$, which has the same units as $X$.

### Pillar 5: Moment Generating Function (MGF) — A Powerful Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (MGF)**
> The **Moment Generating Function** of a discrete random variable $X$ is:
> $$M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}] = \sum_{i} e^{t x_i} p_X(x_i)$$
> defined for values of $t$ in some neighborhood of $0$ where the sum converges.

**Why is the MGF called "moment generating"?**
The $n$-th derivative of $M_X(t)$ evaluated at $t = 0$ gives the $n$-th moment:
$$E[X^n] = M_X^{(n)}(0) = \left.\frac{d^n M_X(t)}{dt^n}\right|_{t=0}$$

Specifically:
- $M_X'(0) = E[X]$ (first moment = mean)
- $M_X''(0) = E[X^2]$ (second raw moment)

### Pillar 6: Skewness and Kurtosis — Shape Descriptors
- **Skewness** measures asymmetry: $\gamma_1 = E\left[\left(\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^3\right]$.
- **Kurtosis** measures "tailedness": $\gamma_2 = E\left[\left(\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^4\right] - 3$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | PMF | $p_X(x_i) = P(X = x_i)$ | $p_X(x_i) \geq 0$, $\sum p_X(x_i) = 1$ |
| 2 | CDF | $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p_X(x_i)$ | Right-continuous, non-decreasing |
| 3 | Interval Probability | $P(a < X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X(a)$ | KTU-favorite identity |
| 4 | Mean (Expected Value) | $E[X] = \sum x_i p_X(x_i)$ | Convergence required |
| 5 | Function of $X$ | $E[g(X)] = \sum g(x_i) p_X(x_i)$ | Linearity: $E[aX+b] = aE[X]+b$ |
| 6 | Variance (Definition) | $\text{Var}(X) = \sum (x_i - \mu)^2 p_X(x_i)$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | Variance (Shortcut) | $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ | Most efficient for KTU |
| 8 | MGF | $M_X(t) = \sum e^{t x_i} p_X(x_i)$ | $M_X(0) = 1$ always |
| 9 | $n$-th moment via MGF | $E[X^n] = M_X^{(n)}(0)$ | Differentiate $n$ times, then $t \to 0$ |
| 10 | Skewness | $\gamma_1 = E\left[\left(\frac{X-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^3\right]$ | Sign indicates direction of skew |
| 11 | Linearity of $E$ | $E[aX + bY + c] = aE[X] + bE[Y] + c$ | No independence required |
| 12 | Variance of linear transform | $\text{Var}(aX + b) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$ | Constant shift $b$ vanishes |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility in CS Engineering**
> - **Quality Control:** Mean and variance of defect counts determine process capability indices ($C_p, C_pk$).
> - **Network Engineering:** Variance of packet delays informs **jitter** calculations and buffer sizing.
> - **Machine Learning:** Cross-entropy loss uses $E[-\log p(X)]$; softmax outputs use PMF of categorical distributions.
> - **Cryptography:** Expected number of trials in a Bernoulli process (e.g., key search) relies on the **Geometric** distribution.

## 2.3 Key Theorem — Law of the Unconscious Statistician (LotUS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (LotUS)**
> If $X$ is a discrete random variable with PMF $p_X(x)$ and $Y = g(X)$ is any function of $X$, then:
> $$E[Y] = E[g(X)] = \sum_{i} g(x_i) \cdot p_X(x_i)$$
> One does **not** need to derive the PMF of $Y$ to compute $E[Y]$ — a frequent source of wasted effort in KTU exams.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Formulas — Engineering Insight

| Question | Engineering Answer |
|----------|-------------------|
| Why $E[X]$? | Predicts **average system load** (servers, packets, users). |
| Why $\text{Var}(X)$? | Measures **risk and uncertainty** in stochastic algorithms. |
| Why MGF? | **Uniquely identifies** a distribution; simplifies sums of independent RVs ($M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) M_Y(t)$). |
| Why CDF? | Used in **hypothesis testing**, **Kolmogorov-Smirnov test**, and **reliability engineering** for system survival analysis. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Shortcut Variance Formula

We will derive $\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2$ from the fundamental definition.

**Step 1:** Write the definition of variance using LotUS.

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[(X - \mu)^2]$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square inside the expectation.

$$(X - \mu)^2 = X^2 - 2\mu X + \mu^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **linearity of expectation** (this does not require independence).

$$E[(X - \mu)^2] = E[X^2] - 2\mu E[X] + E[\mu^2]$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $E[X] = \mu$ (by definition of mean) and that $E[\mu^2] = \mu^2$ (since $\mu$ is a constant).

$$= E[X^2] - 2\mu \cdot \mu + \mu^2$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the algebra.

$$= E[X^2] - 2\mu^2 + \mu^2 = E[X^2] - \mu^2$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back $\mu = E[X]$.

$$\boxed{\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** This shortcut saves **2-3 minutes per KTU problem**, which is the difference between completing the paper and leaving a sub-part blank. The constant $\mu^2$ that appears is **not random** — it is a fixed number once $E[X]$ is computed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Value of a Linear Transformation

**Theorem:** If $Y = aX + b$ for constants $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$, then $E[Y] = aE[X] + b$.

**Step 1:** Apply the definition of expectation to $Y = aX + b$.

$$E[Y] = E[aX + b]$$

**Step 2:** Use LotUS with $g(X) = aX + b$.

$$= \sum_{i} (a x_i + b) \cdot p_X(x_i)$$

**Step 3:** Distribute the sum across the two terms.

$$= a \sum_{i} x_i p_X(x_i) + b \sum_{i} p_X(x_i)$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the first sum as $E[X]$ and the second sum as $1$ (normalization of PMF).

$$= a \cdot E[X] + b \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{E[aX + b] = aE[X] + b}$$

**Step 5:** Similarly derive the variance of the transformation.

$$\text{Var}(aX + b) = E[(aX + b - (a\mu + b))^2] = E[(a(X - \mu))^2] = a^2 E[(X - \mu)^2] = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** The constant $b$ shifts the **location** (affects mean) but **not the spread** (does not affect variance). Only the scaling constant $a$ affects the spread — and it does so **quadratically**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The n-th Moment from the MGF

**Theorem:** $E[X^n] = M_X^{(n)}(0)$.

**Step 1:** Start with the MGF definition.

$$M_X(t) = \sum_{i} e^{t x_i} p_X(x_i)$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$. Apply the chain rule on $e^{t x_i}$, giving $x_i e^{t x_i}$.

$$M_X'(t) = \sum_{i} x_i e^{t x_i} p_X(x_i)$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate at $t = 0$. Note that $e^{0 \cdot x_i} = e^0 = 1$ for all $i$.

$$M_X'(0) = \sum_{i} x_i \cdot 1 \cdot p_X(x_i) = \sum_{i} x_i p_X(x_i) = E[X]$$

**Step 4:** For the second derivative, differentiate $M_X'(t)$ once more.

$$M_X''(t) = \sum_{i} x_i^2 e^{t x_i} p_X(x_i)$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate at $t = 0$.

$$M_X''(0) = \sum_{i} x_i^2 p_X(x_i) = E[X^2]$$

**Step 6:** Inductively, the $n$-th derivative gives:

$$M_X^{(n)}(t) = \sum_{i} x_i^n e^{t x_i} p_X(x_i) \implies M_X^{(n)}(0) = \sum_{i} x_i^n p_X(x_i) = E[X^n]$$

$$\boxed{E[X^n] = M_X^{(n)}(0)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The MGF is the discrete analog of the **Laplace transform**. In control systems, transfer functions use the Laplace transform to characterize system response — the MGF plays the same role in probability.

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Coin Toss Distribution

**Problem:** A fair coin is tossed 3 times. Let $X$ be the number of heads. Find the PMF, CDF, mean, and variance.

**Step 1:** The sample space has $2^3 = 8$ equally likely outcomes. $X \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the PMF using binomial counting.

$$p_X(0) = \frac{\binom{3}{0}}{8} = \frac{1}{8}, \quad p_X(1) = \frac{\binom{3}{1}}{8} = \frac{3}{8}$$

$$p_X(2) = \frac{\binom{3}{2}}{8} = \frac{3}{8}, \quad p_X(3) = \frac{\binom{3}{3}}{8} = \frac{1}{8}$$

**Step 3:** Verify normalization.

$$\frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{8}{8} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Construct the CDF.

$$F_X(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \\ 1/8, & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ 4/8 = 1/2, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ 7/8, & 2 \leq x < 3 \\ 1, & x \geq 3 \end{cases}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $E[X]$.

$$E[X] = 0 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + 1 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 3 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = \frac{0 + 3 + 6 + 3}{8} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5$$

**Step 6:** Compute $E[X^2]$.

$$E[X^2] = 0^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} + 1^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 2^2 \cdot \frac{3}{8} + 3^2 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = \frac{0 + 3 + 12 + 9}{8} = \frac{24}{8} = 3$$

**Step 7:** Apply the variance shortcut.

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = 3 - (1.5)^2 = 3 - 2.25 = 0.75$$

$$\boxed{E[X] = 1.5, \quad \text{Var}(X) = 0.75, \quad \sigma \approx 0.866}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Probability of Interval

**Problem:** Using the same $X$ from Example 1, compute $P(1 \leq X \leq 2)$ and $P(X > 1.5)$.

**Step 1:** Using the CDF.

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = F_X(2) - F_X(0) = \frac{7}{8} - \frac{1}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75$$

**Step 2:** Alternative direct method.

$$P(1 \leq X \leq 2) = p_X(1) + p_X(2) = \frac{3}{8} + \frac{3}{8} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3:** For $P(X > 1.5)$ — since $X$ is integer-valued, $X > 1.5$ is equivalent to $X \geq 2$.

$$P(X > 1.5) = p_X(2) + p_X(3) = \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — How a Discrete Random Variable is Built

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RANDOM EXPERIMENT\n(e.g. Toss 3 coins)"] --> B["SAMPLE SPACE S\n(8 outcomes: HHH, HHT, ...)"]
    B --> C["RANDOM VARIABLE X\nX = number of heads"]
    C --> D["RANGE Rx\n{0, 1, 2, 3}"]
    D --> E["PMF pX(x)\nassigns probability"]
    D --> F["CDF FX(x)\ncumulative sum"]
    E --> G["MOMENTS\nMean, Variance, MGF"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["ENGINEERING DECISIONS\nCapacity, Risk, QoS"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Information Flow from Experiment to Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        E1["Experiment"]
    end
    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1["Define X on S"]
        P2["Compute PMF"]
        P3["Compute CDF"]
    end
    subgraph ANAL["ANALYTICS LAYER"]
        A1["Expected Value"]
        A2["Variance"]
        A3["MGF / Moments"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["Prediction"]
        O2["Risk Profile"]
        O3["Design Decision"]
    end
    E1 --> P1 --> P2 --> A1 --> O1
    E1 --> P1 --> P2 --> A2 --> O2
    E1 --> P1 --> P3 --> A3 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Tree Diagram — Three Coin Tosses (Visual Sample Space)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["TOSS 1"] --> L1A["H"]
    ROOT --> L1B["T"]
    L1A --> L2A["H"]
    L1A --> L2B["T"]
    L1B --> L2C["H"]
    L1B --> L2D["T"]
    L2A --> L3A["H = 3 heads"]
    L2A --> L3B["T = 2 heads"]
    L2B --> L3C["H = 2 heads"]
    L2B --> L3D["T = 1 head"]
    L2C --> L3E["H = 1 head"]
    L2C --> L3F["T = 0 heads"]
    L2D --> L3G["H = 0 heads"]
    L2D --> L3H["T -- invalid leaf"]
```

## 4.4 PMF-to-CDF Transformation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PMF["DISCRETE PMF"]
        X0["x = 0 -> p = 1/8"]
        X1["x = 1 -> p = 3/8"]
        X2["x = 2 -> p = 3/8"]
        X3["x = 3 -> p = 1/8"]
    end
    subgraph CDF["STEP CDF"]
        Y0["F(0) = 1/8"]
        Y1["F(1) = 4/8"]
        Y2["F(2) = 7/8"]
        Y3["F(3) = 1"]
    end
    X0 -->|"sum 0 to 0"| Y0
    X0 -->|"sum 0 to 1"| Y1
    X0 -->|"sum 0 to 2"| Y2
    X0 -->|"sum 0 to 3"| Y3
    X1 --> Y1
    X1 --> Y2
    X1 --> Y3
    X2 --> Y2
    X2 --> Y3
    X3 --> Y3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams in the Exam Hall:** When a KTU question describes a probability distribution in words (e.g., "the number of defective items in a batch of 5"), the **first thing to draw** is a **probability table** (essentially the PMF table). This is worth 2-3 marks by itself, even before any calculation.
