# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a counting process. State the four axioms that $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ must satisfy to qualify as a counting process.**

**Model Answer:**
A **counting process** is a stochastic process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ that records the cumulative number of events that have occurred up to time $t$. The four axioms are:
1. $N(0) = 0$ — process starts at zero.
2. $N(t)$ is integer-valued and non-negative: $N(t) \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$.
3. $N(t)$ is non-decreasing: if $s < t$, then $N(s) \leq N(t)$.
4. $N(t)$ is right-continuous: $\lim_{s \to t^+} N(s) = N(t)$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the relationship between the Poisson counting process and the exponential inter-arrival times.**

**Model Answer:**
If $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$, then the inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, X_3, \dots$ are **independent and identically distributed** random variables, each following an **exponential distribution** with parameter $\lambda$. Conversely, if the inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$), then the counting process is Poisson with rate $\lambda$. This is a **two-way equivalence (iff relationship)**.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**(a)** Customers arrive at a bank according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = 4$ customers per hour. Compute:
- (i) The probability that exactly 3 customers arrive in the first 30 minutes. **[3 marks]**
- (ii) The expected number of customers in 2 hours and the variance. **[2 marks]**
- (iii) The probability that the time between two consecutive arrivals is between 10 and 20 minutes. **[2 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the Poisson PMF starting from the assumption that the inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$). Show all steps explicitly. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i)** Here $t = 0.5$ hours, $\lambda t = 4 \cdot 0.5 = 2$, $n = 3$.
$$P\{N(0.5) = 3\} = \frac{2^3 e^{-2}}{3!} = \frac{8 e^{-2}}{6} = \frac{4 e^{-2}}{3} \approx 0.1804$$

**(ii)** For $t = 2$ hours:
$$E[N(2)] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \text{ customers}, \quad \text{Var}[N(2)] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8$$

**(iii)** Inter-arrival $X \sim \text{Exp}(4)$ (rate per hour). We need $P\{1/6 \leq X \leq 1/3\}$:
$$P\{1/6 \leq X \leq 1/3\} = F(1/3) - F(1/6) = \left(1 - e^{-4/3}\right) - \left(1 - e^{-4/6}\right)$$
$$= e^{-2/3} - e^{-4/3} \approx 0.5134 - 0.2636 = 0.2498$$

#### Model Solution for (b): The Derivation

This is a complete re-derivation of the result. Follow the steps from Section 3.1 above:

1. Define inter-arrival times $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, i.i.d.
2. Define arrival times $T_n = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ (Erlang/Gamma distributed).
3. Use the equivalence $\{N(t) \geq n\} = \{T_n \leq t\}$.
4. Derive the Erlang CDF:
$$F_{T_n}(t) = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$
5. Use the telescoping difference $P\{N(t) = n\} = P\{N(t) \geq n\} - P\{N(t) \geq n+1\}$ to obtain the final result:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write $P\{T_n \leq t\}$ directly as $\frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$ — this is **WRONG**. $P\{T_n \leq t\}$ is the **CDF** of a Gamma distribution, which equals a *tail sum* of Poisson probabilities, not a single Poisson PMF. The final Poisson PMF is obtained by the **telescoping difference** of two such tail sums. Loses 2-3 marks if done incorrectly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a)** Define a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$. Prove that for a Poisson process, $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$ and $\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Software bugs are reported to a project manager at a constant average rate of 2 bugs per day, modeled as a Poisson process. Find:
- (i) The probability that no bugs are reported on a given day. **[2 marks]**
- (ii) The probability that at least 5 bugs are reported in 3 days. **[3 marks]**
- (iii) The probability that the time to the first bug exceeds 1 day. **[2 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** A counting process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$ if:
- $N(0) = 0$.
- It has independent increments.
- $N(t)$ is Poisson distributed: $P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$.

**Proof of Mean:** Using the definition of expectation for a Poisson random variable:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!} = (\lambda t) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$$

Letting $m = n - 1$:
$$E[N(t)] = (\lambda t) \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^m e^{-\lambda t}}{m!} = (\lambda t) \cdot e^{\lambda t} e^{-\lambda t} = \lambda t$$

**Proof of Variance:** Using $E[N(t)^2] = E[N(t)(N(t)-1)] + E[N(t)]$:
$$E[N(t)(N(t)-1)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n(n-1) \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!} = (\lambda t)^2 \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-2} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-2)!} = (\lambda t)^2$$

Therefore:
$$\text{Var}[N(t)] = E[N(t)^2] - (E[N(t)])^2 = \left[(\lambda t)^2 + \lambda t\right] - (\lambda t)^2 = \lambda t$$

$$\boxed{E[N(t)] = \text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i)** With $\lambda = 2$ bugs/day, $t = 1$ day, $n = 0$:
$$P\{N(1) = 0\} = \frac{(2 \cdot 1)^0 e^{-2}}{0!} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**(ii)** With $\lambda = 2$, $t = 3$ days, $\lambda t = 6$:
$$P\{N(3) \geq 5\} = 1 - P\{N(3) \leq 4\} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{4} \frac{6^k e^{-6}}{k!}$$

Compute each term:
- $k=0$: $e^{-6} \approx 0.00248$
- $k=1$: $6 e^{-6} \approx 0.01487$
- $k=2$: $18 e^{-6} \approx 0.04462$
- $k=3$: $36 e^{-6} \approx 0.08924$
- $k=4$: $54 e^{-6} \approx 0.13385$

Sum $\approx 0.28506$, so $P\{N(3) \geq 5\} \approx 1 - 0.2851 = 0.7149$.

**(iii)** Time to first bug $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(2)$. We need $P\{X_1 > 1\}$:
$$P\{X_1 > 1\} = e^{-2 \cdot 1} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In (ii), students often forget to compute $1 - P\{N(3) \leq 4\}$ and instead try to directly sum $k=5$ to $\infty$, which is *infinite*. Always use the **complement rule** when asked for "at least" probabilities. Loses 2 marks for this oversight.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Counting Process Definition:** A stochastic process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ that counts the cumulative number of events up to time $t$. Must satisfy $N(0)=0$, integer-valued, non-decreasing, and right-continuous.
- **Poisson Process:** The *only* counting process with **independent and stationary** increments where event counts follow the Poisson distribution $P\{N(t)=n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$.
- **Mean = Variance = $\lambda t$:** A signature property of the Poisson process. Use it to verify solutions and for sanity checks.
- **Inter-arrival Duality:** Poisson($\lambda$) process $\Leftrightarrow$ i.i.d. Exponential($\lambda$) inter-arrival times. This is the **iff** relationship that is the heart of KTU 14-mark problems.
- **Erlang / Gamma Connection:** $T_n = X_1 + \dots + X_n$ follows the Erlang distribution with PDF $\frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$.
- **Memoryless Property:** $P\{X > s + t \mid X > t\} = P\{X > s\}$ — only true for the exponential distribution, making it ideal for modeling "random" events.
- **Engineering Applications:** Use Poisson processes for modeling **arrivals** (packets, customers, phone calls, hardware failures, software bugs, radioactive decay).
- **Complement Rule:** For "at least $n$" probabilities, always use $P\{N(t) \geq n\} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} P\{N(t) = k\}$.
- **Stationarity vs Independence:** Independence applies to *disjoint* intervals; stationarity means the distribution depends only on interval *length*.
- **KTU Style Reminder:** Always state the axioms of a counting process explicitly before applying the Poisson PMF. Examiners award partial marks for clear definitions.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Counting Process

A **stochastic process** $\{N(t),\, t \geq 0\}$ is called a **counting process** if it represents the total number of events (or "arrivals") that have occurred in the time interval $[0,\, t]$. Formally, it must satisfy the following axiomatic properties used in KTU Board Examinations:

1. **Initial Condition:** $N(0) = 0$ (no events have occurred at time zero).
2. **Non-negativity:** $N(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$.
3. **Integer-Valued State Space:** $N(t) \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ (the process can only take non-negative integer values).
4. **Monotonicity / Non-decreasing:** If $s < t$, then $N(s) \leq N(t)$ (events are cumulative and cannot be "un-counted").
5. **Right-Continuity:** The function $t \mapsto N(t)$ is right-continuous, i.e., $\lim_{s \to t^+} N(s) = N(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** A counting process is essentially a *step function* that jumps by **+1** at every event occurrence. The state space is always the set of non-negative integers $\mathbb{N}_0 = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting at a **busy traffic intersection in Kochi** with a tally counter in your hand. Every time a car passes, you press the button on your counter.

- Your counter reading at time $t$ is exactly $N(t)$.
- $N(t)$ can only go **up** — once a car has passed, you cannot "un-pass" it (this gives us **monotonicity**).
- $N(t)$ must always be a **whole number** — you cannot have 4.7 cars pass.
- $N(0) = 0$ — at the start, no cars have passed.

This tally counter is the perfect real-world embodiment of a **counting process**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Counting processes are the mathematical backbone of **queueing theory**, **reliability engineering**, **network traffic modeling**, and **hardware failure analysis** — all of which are core areas in Computer Science and Information Technology.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Properties of a Counting Process

Beyond the axiomatic definition, two *incremental properties* govern how a counting process evolves. These are heavily tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

### Property A: Independent Increments
A counting process has **independent increments** if the number of events occurring in **disjoint** time intervals are mutually independent random variables.

Mathematically, for any $0 = t_0 < t_1 < t_2 < \dots < t_n$, the random variables
$$N(t_1) - N(t_0),\; N(t_2) - N(t_1),\; \dots,\; N(t_n) - N(t_{n-1})$$
are **mutually independent**.

### Property B: Stationary Increments
A counting process has **stationary increments** if the distribution of the number of events in any interval depends *only* on the **length** of the interval, not on its starting point.

Mathematically, $N(t + s) - N(s)$ has the same distribution as $N(t) - N(0) = N(t)$ for all $s, t \geq 0$.

## 2.2 The Poisson Process — The King of Counting Processes

A counting process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is called a **Poisson Process** with rate $\lambda > 0$ if:

1. $N(0) = 0$.
2. It has **independent increments**.
3. The number of events in any interval of length $t$ follows a **Poisson distribution** with mean $\lambda t$:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trigger Word:** Whenever the problem says "events occur at a constant average rate $\lambda$", the answer is almost always a **Poisson Process**.

## 2.3 Inter-Arrival Times

Let $T_1, T_2, T_3, \dots$ be the time of the first, second, third, … event. Define the **inter-arrival times** as
$$X_n = T_n - T_{n-1}, \quad n \geq 1 \quad (\text{with } T_0 = 0).$$

For a **Poisson process with rate $\lambda$**:
- Each $X_n$ is **independent**.
- Each $X_n$ is **exponentially distributed** with parameter $\lambda$ and mean $1/\lambda$.
- Probability Density Function (PDF):
$$f_{X_n}(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$
- Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):
$$P\{X_n \leq x\} = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property of Exponential Distribution:** $P\{X > s + t \mid X > t\} = P\{X > s\}$. This is the *only* continuous distribution with this property, and it is the reason Poisson processes model "random" event arrivals so well.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Key Parameter** | **Engineering Use** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Poisson PMF | $P\{N(t)=n\} = \dfrac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$ | $n \in \mathbb{N}_0$ | Event count probability |
| 2 | Mean of Poisson Process | $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$ | rate $\lambda > 0$ | Expected arrivals |
| 3 | Variance of Poisson Process | $\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$ | — | Spread of arrivals |
| 4 | Exponential PDF (Inter-arrival) | $f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Time-between-events |
| 5 | Exponential CDF | $F_X(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Reliability / survival |
| 6 | Survival Function | $P\{X > x\} = e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Hazard analysis |
| 7 | Mean Inter-arrival Time | $E[X] = 1/\lambda$ | minutes/event | Throughput design |
| 8 | Sum of Exponentials (Gamma) | $f_{T_n}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$ | $t \geq 0$ | Time of $n$-th event |
| 9 | Hazard Rate | $r(t) = \lambda$ (constant) | $t \geq 0$ | Constant failure rate |
| 10 | Conditional Probability | $P\{N(t+s)=n+m \mid N(s)=n\} = P\{N(t)=m\}$ | stationary increments | Memoryless behavior |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Inter-Arrival Times to Poisson Process

**Goal:** Show that if the inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, \dots$ are i.i.d. **exponential with rate $\lambda$**, then the number of events $N(t)$ in $[0,\,t]$ is **Poisson with mean $\lambda t$**.

### Step 1 — Define the Event Count
The event $\{N(t) \geq n\}$ means that the $n$-th event has occurred at or before time $t$. That is,
$$\{N(t) \geq n\} = \{T_n \leq t\}$$
where $T_n = X_1 + X_2 + \dots + X_n$ is the sum of the first $n$ inter-arrival times (also called the **$n$-th order statistic** of the arrival times).

### Step 2 — Compute the CDF of $T_n$
Since $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, the sum $T_n$ follows a **Gamma (Erlang) distribution** with shape $n$ and rate $\lambda$. The PDF of $T_n$ is given by convolution:
$$f_{T_n}(t) = \int_0^t f_{X_{n}}(t - s) f_{T_{n-1}}(s)\, ds$$

By induction on $n$, we obtain:
$$f_{T_n}(t) = \frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}, \quad t \geq 0$$

### Step 3 — Derive the CDF via Integration
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \int_0^t \frac{\lambda^n s^{n-1} e^{-\lambda s}}{(n-1)!}\, ds$$

Performing a change of variable $u = \lambda s$, so $du = \lambda\, ds$:
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \int_0^{\lambda t} \frac{u^{n-1} e^{-u}}{(n-1)!}\, du$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Incomplete Gamma Function
This integral is the **regularized upper incomplete Gamma function** $1 - \Gamma(n, \lambda t)/(n-1)!$. By the **Poisson–Gamma relationship** (also called the *Poisson–Erlang duality*):
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

### Step 5 — Invert the Relation to Get $P\{N(t) = n\}$
We know that
$$P\{N(t) \geq n\} = P\{T_n \leq t\}$$

Therefore,
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = P\{N(t) \geq n\} - P\{N(t) \geq n+1\}$$

This gives:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} - \sum_{k=n+1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

$$\boxed{P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$) **if and only if** the counting process is a **Poisson process with rate $\lambda$**. This is the single most important duality in KTU Board examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of $N(t)$

### Mean
Using the fact that $E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P\{N(t) > n\}$ and $P\{N(t) > n\} = P\{T_{n+1} \leq t\}$:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P\{T_{n+1} \leq t\} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

Interchanging the order of summation:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} \cdot k = \lambda t \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{k-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(k-1)!}$$

Letting $j = k-1$:
$$E[N(t)] = \lambda t \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^j e^{-\lambda t}}{j!} = \lambda t \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{E[N(t)] = \lambda t}$$

### Variance
A similar (lengthier) computation using $\text{Var}[N(t)] = E[N(t)^2] - (E[N(t)])^2$ and the Taylor expansion of $e^{\lambda t}$ yields:
$$\boxed{\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *For a Poisson process, the mean always equals the variance, and both equal $\lambda t$.* This is a unique property — no other common counting process has this equality.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Packets arrive at a router according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = 3$ packets/second. Find:
1. The probability that exactly 2 packets arrive in the first second.
2. The expected number of packets in 5 seconds.
3. The probability that the time between consecutive packets is more than 0.5 seconds.

**Solution to (1):**
$$P\{N(1) = 2\} = \frac{(3 \cdot 1)^2 e^{-3 \cdot 1}}{2!} = \frac{9 e^{-3}}{2} \approx 0.2240$$

**Solution to (2):**
$$E[N(5)] = 3 \cdot 5 = 15 \text{ packets}$$

**Solution to (3):** The inter-arrival time $X \sim \text{Exp}(3)$. We need $P\{X > 0.5\}$:
$$P\{X > 0.5\} = e^{-3 \cdot 0.5} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a counting process. State the four axioms that $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ must satisfy to qualify as a counting process.**

**Model Answer:**
A **counting process** is a stochastic process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ that records the cumulative number of events that have occurred up to time $t$. The four axioms are:
1. $N(0) = 0$ — process starts at zero.
2. $N(t)$ is integer-valued and non-negative: $N(t) \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$.
3. $N(t)$ is non-decreasing: if $s < t$, then $N(s) \leq N(t)$.
4. $N(t)$ is right-continuous: $\lim_{s \to t^+} N(s) = N(t)$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the relationship between the Poisson counting process and the exponential inter-arrival times.**

**Model Answer:**
If $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$, then the inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, X_3, \dots$ are **independent and identically distributed** random variables, each following an **exponential distribution** with parameter $\lambda$. Conversely, if the inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$), then the counting process is Poisson with rate $\lambda$. This is a **two-way equivalence (iff relationship)**.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**(a)** Customers arrive at a bank according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = 4$ customers per hour. Compute:
- (i) The probability that exactly 3 customers arrive in the first 30 minutes. **[3 marks]**
- (ii) The expected number of customers in 2 hours and the variance. **[2 marks]**
- (iii) The probability that the time between two consecutive arrivals is between 10 and 20 minutes. **[2 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the Poisson PMF starting from the assumption that the inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$). Show all steps explicitly. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i)** Here $t = 0.5$ hours, $\lambda t = 4 \cdot 0.5 = 2$, $n = 3$.
$$P\{N(0.5) = 3\} = \frac{2^3 e^{-2}}{3!} = \frac{8 e^{-2}}{6} = \frac{4 e^{-2}}{3} \approx 0.1804$$

**(ii)** For $t = 2$ hours:
$$E[N(2)] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \text{ customers}, \quad \text{Var}[N(2)] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8$$

**(iii)** Inter-arrival $X \sim \text{Exp}(4)$ (rate per hour). We need $P\{1/6 \leq X \leq 1/3\}$:
$$P\{1/6 \leq X \leq 1/3\} = F(1/3) - F(1/6) = \left(1 - e^{-4/3}\right) - \left(1 - e^{-4/6}\right)$$
$$= e^{-2/3} - e^{-4/3} \approx 0.5134 - 0.2636 = 0.2498$$

#### Model Solution for (b): The Derivation

This is a complete re-derivation of the result. Follow the steps from Section 3.1 above:

1. Define inter-arrival times $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, i.i.d.
2. Define arrival times $T_n = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ (Erlang/Gamma distributed).
3. Use the equivalence $\{N(t) \geq n\} = \{T_n \leq t\}$.
4. Derive the Erlang CDF:
$$F_{T_n}(t) = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$
5. Use the telescoping difference $P\{N(t) = n\} = P\{N(t) \geq n\} - P\{N(t) \geq n+1\}$ to obtain the final result:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write $P\{T_n \leq t\}$ directly as $\frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$ — this is **WRONG**. $P\{T_n \leq t\}$ is the **CDF** of a Gamma distribution, which equals a *tail sum* of Poisson probabilities, not a single Poisson PMF. The final Poisson PMF is obtained by the **telescoping difference** of two such tail sums. Loses 2-3 marks if done incorrectly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a)** Define a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$. Prove that for a Poisson process, $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$ and $\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Software bugs are reported to a project manager at a constant average rate of 2 bugs per day, modeled as a Poisson process. Find:
- (i) The probability that no bugs are reported on a given day. **[2 marks]**
- (ii) The probability that at least 5 bugs are reported in 3 days. **[3 marks]**
- (iii) The probability that the time to the first bug exceeds 1 day. **[2 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** A counting process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$ if:
- $N(0) = 0$.
- It has independent increments.
- $N(t)$ is Poisson distributed: $P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$.

**Proof of Mean:** Using the definition of expectation for a Poisson random variable:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!} = (\lambda t) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$$

Letting $m = n - 1$:
$$E[N(t)] = (\lambda t) \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^m e^{-\lambda t}}{m!} = (\lambda t) \cdot e^{\lambda t} e^{-\lambda t} = \lambda t$$

**Proof of Variance:** Using $E[N(t)^2] = E[N(t)(N(t)-1)] + E[N(t)]$:
$$E[N(t)(N(t)-1)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n(n-1) \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!} = (\lambda t)^2 \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-2} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-2)!} = (\lambda t)^2$$

Therefore:
$$\text{Var}[N(t)] = E[N(t)^2] - (E[N(t)])^2 = \left[(\lambda t)^2 + \lambda t\right] - (\lambda t)^2 = \lambda t$$

$$\boxed{E[N(t)] = \text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i)** With $\lambda = 2$ bugs/day, $t = 1$ day, $n = 0$:
$$P\{N(1) = 0\} = \frac{(2 \cdot 1)^0 e^{-2}}{0!} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**(ii)** With $\lambda = 2$, $t = 3$ days, $\lambda t = 6$:
$$P\{N(3) \geq 5\} = 1 - P\{N(3) \leq 4\} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{4} \frac{6^k e^{-6}}{k!}$$

Compute each term:
- $k=0$: $e^{-6} \approx 0.00248$
- $k=1$: $6 e^{-6} \approx 0.01487$
- $k=2$: $18 e^{-6} \approx 0.04462$
- $k=3$: $36 e^{-6} \approx 0.08924$
- $k=4$: $54 e^{-6} \approx 0.13385$

Sum $\approx 0.28506$, so $P\{N(3) \geq 5\} \approx 1 - 0.2851 = 0.7149$.

**(iii)** Time to first bug $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(2)$. We need $P\{X_1 > 1\}$:
$$P\{X_1 > 1\} = e^{-2 \cdot 1} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In (ii), students often forget to compute $1 - P\{N(3) \leq 4\}$ and instead try to directly sum $k=5$ to $\infty$, which is *infinite*. Always use the **complement rule** when asked for "at least" probabilities. Loses 2 marks for this oversight.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Counting Process Definition:** A stochastic process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ that counts the cumulative number of events up to time $t$. Must satisfy $N(0)=0$, integer-valued, non-decreasing, and right-continuous.
- **Poisson Process:** The *only* counting process with **independent and stationary** increments where event counts follow the Poisson distribution $P\{N(t)=n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$.
- **Mean = Variance = $\lambda t$:** A signature property of the Poisson process. Use it to verify solutions and for sanity checks.
- **Inter-arrival Duality:** Poisson($\lambda$) process $\Leftrightarrow$ i.i.d. Exponential($\lambda$) inter-arrival times. This is the **iff** relationship that is the heart of KTU 14-mark problems.
- **Erlang / Gamma Connection:** $T_n = X_1 + \dots + X_n$ follows the Erlang distribution with PDF $\frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$.
- **Memoryless Property:** $P\{X > s + t \mid X > t\} = P\{X > s\}$ — only true for the exponential distribution, making it ideal for modeling "random" events.
- **Engineering Applications:** Use Poisson processes for modeling **arrivals** (packets, customers, phone calls, hardware failures, software bugs, radioactive decay).
- **Complement Rule:** For "at least $n$" probabilities, always use $P\{N(t) \geq n\} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} P\{N(t) = k\}$.
- **Stationarity vs Independence:** Independence applies to *disjoint* intervals; stationarity means the distribution depends only on interval *length*.
- **KTU Style Reminder:** Always state the axioms of a counting process explicitly before applying the Poisson PMF. Examiners award partial marks for clear definitions.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Counting Process

A **stochastic process** $\{N(t),\, t \geq 0\}$ is called a **counting process** if it represents the total number of events (or "arrivals") that have occurred in the time interval $[0,\, t]$. Formally, it must satisfy the following axiomatic properties used in KTU Board Examinations:

1. **Initial Condition:** $N(0) = 0$ (no events have occurred at time zero).
2. **Non-negativity:** $N(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$.
3. **Integer-Valued State Space:** $N(t) \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ (the process can only take non-negative integer values).
4. **Monotonicity / Non-decreasing:** If $s < t$, then $N(s) \leq N(t)$ (events are cumulative and cannot be "un-counted").
5. **Right-Continuity:** The function $t \mapsto N(t)$ is right-continuous, i.e., $\lim_{s \to t^+} N(s) = N(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** A counting process is essentially a *step function* that jumps by **+1** at every event occurrence. The state space is always the set of non-negative integers $\mathbb{N}_0 = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting at a **busy traffic intersection in Kochi** with a tally counter in your hand. Every time a car passes, you press the button on your counter.

- Your counter reading at time $t$ is exactly $N(t)$.
- $N(t)$ can only go **up** — once a car has passed, you cannot "un-pass" it (this gives us **monotonicity**).
- $N(t)$ must always be a **whole number** — you cannot have 4.7 cars pass.
- $N(0) = 0$ — at the start, no cars have passed.

This tally counter is the perfect real-world embodiment of a **counting process**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Counting processes are the mathematical backbone of **queueing theory**, **reliability engineering**, **network traffic modeling**, and **hardware failure analysis** — all of which are core areas in Computer Science and Information Technology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Properties of a Counting Process

Beyond the axiomatic definition, two *incremental properties* govern how a counting process evolves. These are heavily tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

### Property A: Independent Increments
A counting process has **independent increments** if the number of events occurring in **disjoint** time intervals are mutually independent random variables.

Mathematically, for any $0 = t_0 < t_1 < t_2 < \dots < t_n$, the random variables
$$N(t_1) - N(t_0),\; N(t_2) - N(t_1),\; \dots,\; N(t_n) - N(t_{n-1})$$
are **mutually independent**.

### Property B: Stationary Increments
A counting process has **stationary increments** if the distribution of the number of events in any interval depends *only* on the **length** of the interval, not on its starting point.

Mathematically, $N(t + s) - N(s)$ has the same distribution as $N(t) - N(0) = N(t)$ for all $s, t \geq 0$.

## 2.2 The Poisson Process — The King of Counting Processes

A counting process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is called a **Poisson Process** with rate $\lambda > 0$ if:

1. $N(0) = 0$.
2. It has **independent increments**.
3. The number of events in any interval of length $t$ follows a **Poisson distribution** with mean $\lambda t$:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trigger Word:** Whenever the problem says "events occur at a constant average rate $\lambda$", the answer is almost always a **Poisson Process**.

## 2.3 Inter-Arrival Times

Let $T_1, T_2, T_3, \dots$ be the time of the first, second, third, … event. Define the **inter-arrival times** as
$$X_n = T_n - T_{n-1}, \quad n \geq 1 \quad (\text{with } T_0 = 0).$$

For a **Poisson process with rate $\lambda$**:
- Each $X_n$ is **independent**.
- Each $X_n$ is **exponentially distributed** with parameter $\lambda$ and mean $1/\lambda$.
- Probability Density Function (PDF):
$$f_{X_n}(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$
- Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):
$$P\{X_n \leq x\} = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property of Exponential Distribution:** $P\{X > s + t \mid X > t\} = P\{X > s\}$. This is the *only* continuous distribution with this property, and it is the reason Poisson processes model "random" event arrivals so well.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Key Parameter** | **Engineering Use** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Poisson PMF | $P\{N(t)=n\} = \dfrac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$ | $n \in \mathbb{N}_0$ | Event count probability |
| 2 | Mean of Poisson Process | $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$ | rate $\lambda > 0$ | Expected arrivals |
| 3 | Variance of Poisson Process | $\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$ | — | Spread of arrivals |
| 4 | Exponential PDF (Inter-arrival) | $f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Time-between-events |
| 5 | Exponential CDF | $F_X(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Reliability / survival |
| 6 | Survival Function | $P\{X > x\} = e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Hazard analysis |
| 7 | Mean Inter-arrival Time | $E[X] = 1/\lambda$ | minutes/event | Throughput design |
| 8 | Sum of Exponentials (Gamma) | $f_{T_n}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$ | $t \geq 0$ | Time of $n$-th event |
| 9 | Hazard Rate | $r(t) = \lambda$ (constant) | $t \geq 0$ | Constant failure rate |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Inter-Arrival Times to Poisson Process

**Goal:** Show that if the inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, \dots$ are i.i.d. **exponential with rate $\lambda$**, then the number of events $N(t)$ in $[0,\,t]$ is **Poisson with mean $\lambda t$**.

### Step 1 — Define the Event Count
The event $\{N(t) \geq n\}$ means that the $n$-th event has occurred at or before time $t$. That is,
$$\{N(t) \geq n\} = \{T_n \leq t\}$$
where $T_n = X_1 + X_2 + \dots + X_n$ is the sum of the first $n$ inter-arrival times (also called the **$n$-th order statistic** of the arrival times).

### Step 2 — Compute the CDF of $T_n$
Since $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, the sum $T_n$ follows a **Gamma (Erlang) distribution** with shape $n$ and rate $\lambda$. The PDF of $T_n$ is given by convolution:
$$f_{T_n}(t) = \int_0^t f_{X_{n}}(t - s) f_{T_{n-1}}(s)\, ds$$

By induction on $n$, we obtain:
$$f_{T_n}(t) = \frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}, \quad t \geq 0$$

### Step 3 — Derive the CDF via Integration
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \int_0^t \frac{\lambda^n s^{n-1} e^{-\lambda s}}{(n-1)!}\, ds$$

Performing a change of variable $u = \lambda s$, so $du = \lambda\, ds$:
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \int_0^{\lambda t} \frac{u^{n-1} e^{-u}}{(n-1)!}\, du$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Incomplete Gamma Function
This integral is the **regularized upper incomplete Gamma function** $1 - \Gamma(n, \lambda t)/(n-1)!$. By the **Poisson–Gamma relationship** (also called the *Poisson–Erlang duality*):
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

### Step 5 — Invert the Relation to Get $P\{N(t) = n\}$
We know that
$$P\{N(t) \geq n\} = P\{T_n \leq t\}$$

Therefore,
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = P\{N(t) \geq n\} - P\{N(t) \geq n+1\}$$

This gives:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} - \sum_{k=n+1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

$$\boxed{P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$) **if and only if** the counting process is a **Poisson process with rate $\lambda$**. This is the single most important duality in KTU Board examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of $N(t)$

### Mean
Using the fact that $E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P\{N(t) > n\}$ and $P\{N(t) > n\} = P\{T_{n+1} \leq t\}$:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P\{T_{n+1} \leq t\} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

Interchanging the order of summation:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} \cdot k = \lambda t \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{k-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(k-1)!}$$

Letting $j = k-1$:
$$E[N(t)] = \lambda t \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^j e^{-\lambda t}}{j!} = \lambda t \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{E[N(t)] = \lambda t}$$

### Variance
A similar (lengthier) computation using $\text{Var}[N(t)] = E[N(t)^2] - (E[N(t)])^2$ and the Taylor expansion of $e^{\lambda t}$ yields:
$$\boxed{\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *For a Poisson process, the mean always equals the variance, and both equal $\lambda t$.* This is a unique property — no other common counting process has this equality.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Packets arrive at a router according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = 3$ packets/second. Find:
1. The probability that exactly 2 packets arrive in the first second.
2. The expected number of packets in 5 seconds.
3. The probability that the time between consecutive packets is more than 0.5 seconds.

**Solution to (1):**
$$P\{N(1) = 2\} = \frac{(3 \cdot 1)^2 e^{-3 \cdot 1}}{2!} = \frac{9 e^{-3}}{2} \approx 0.2240$$

**Solution to (2):**
$$E[N(5)] = 3 \cdot 5 = 15 \text{ packets}$$

**Solution to (3):** The inter-arrival time $X \sim \text{Exp}(3)$. We need $P\{X > 0.5\}$:
$$P\{X > 0.5\} = e^{-3 \cdot 0.5} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a counting process. State the four axioms that $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ must satisfy to qualify as a counting process.**

**Model Answer:**
A **counting process** is a stochastic process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ that records the cumulative number of events that have occurred up to time $t$. The four axioms are:
1. $N(0) = 0$ — process starts at zero.
2. $N(t)$ is integer-valued and non-negative: $N(t) \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$.
3. $N(t)$ is non-decreasing: if $s < t$, then $N(s) \leq N(t)$.
4. $N(t)$ is right-continuous: $\lim_{s \to t^+} N(s) = N(t)$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the relationship between the Poisson counting process and the exponential inter-arrival times.**

**Model Answer:**
If $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$, then the inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, X_3, \dots$ are **independent and identically distributed** random variables, each following an **exponential distribution** with parameter $\lambda$. Conversely, if the inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$), then the counting process is Poisson with rate $\lambda$. This is a **two-way equivalence (iff relationship)**.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**(a)** Customers arrive at a bank according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = 4$ customers per hour. Compute:
- (i) The probability that exactly 3 customers arrive in the first 30 minutes. **[3 marks]**
- (ii) The expected number of customers in 2 hours and the variance. **[2 marks]**
- (iii) The probability that the time between two consecutive arrivals is between 10 and 20 minutes. **[2 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the Poisson PMF starting from the assumption that the inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$). Show all steps explicitly. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i)** Here $t = 0.5$ hours, $\lambda t = 4 \cdot 0.5 = 2$, $n = 3$.
$$P\{N(0.5) = 3\} = \frac{2^3 e^{-2}}{3!} = \frac{8 e^{-2}}{6} = \frac{4 e^{-2}}{3} \approx 0.1804$$

**(ii)** For $t = 2$ hours:
$$E[N(2)] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \text{ customers}, \quad \text{Var}[N(2)] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8$$

**(iii)** Inter-arrival $X \sim \text{Exp}(4)$ (rate per hour). We need $P\{1/6 \leq X \leq 1/3\}$:
$$P\{1/6 \leq X \leq 1/3\} = F(1/3) - F(1/6) = \left(1 - e^{-4/3}\right) - \left(1 - e^{-4/6}\right)$$
$$= e^{-2/3} - e^{-4/3} \approx 0.5134 - 0.2636 = 0.2498$$

#### Model Solution for (b): The Derivation

This is a complete re-derivation of the result. Follow the steps from Section 3.1 above:

1. Define inter-arrival times $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, i.i.d.
2. Define arrival times $T_n = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ (Erlang/Gamma distributed).
3. Use the equivalence $\{N(t) \geq n\} = \{T_n \leq t\}$.
4. Derive the Erlang CDF:
$$F_{T_n}(t) = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$
5. Use the telescoping difference $P\{N(t) = n\} = P\{N(t) \geq n\} - P\{N(t) \geq n+1\}$ to obtain the final result:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write $P\{T_n \leq t\}$ directly as $\frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$ — this is **WRONG**. $P\{T_n \leq t\}$ is the **CDF** of a Gamma distribution, which equals a *tail sum* of Poisson probabilities, not a single Poisson PMF. The final Poisson PMF is obtained by the **telescoping difference** of two such tail sums. Loses 2-3 marks if done incorrectly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a)** Define a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$. Prove that for a Poisson process, $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$ and $\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Software bugs are reported to a project manager at a constant average rate of 2 bugs per day, modeled as a Poisson process. Find:
- (i) The probability that no bugs are reported on a given day. **[2 marks]**
- (ii) The probability that at least 5 bugs are reported in 3 days. **[3 marks]**
- (iii) The probability that the time to the first bug exceeds 1 day. **[2 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** A counting process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$ if:
- $N(0) = 0$.
- It has independent increments.
- $N(t)$ is Poisson distributed: $P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$.

**Proof of Mean:** Using the definition of expectation for a Poisson random variable:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!} = (\lambda t) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$$

Letting $m = n - 1$:
$$E[N(t)] = (\lambda t) \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^m e^{-\lambda t}}{m!} = (\lambda t) \cdot e^{\lambda t} e^{-\lambda t} = \lambda t$$

**Proof of Variance:** Using $E[N(t)^2] = E[N(t)(N(t)-1)] + E[N(t)]$:
$$E[N(t)(N(t)-1)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n(n-1) \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!} = (\lambda t)^2 \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-2} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-2)!} = (\lambda t)^2$$

Therefore:
$$\text{Var}[N(t)] = E[N(t)^2] - (E[N(t)])^2 = \left[(\lambda t)^2 + \lambda t\right] - (\lambda t)^2 = \lambda t$$

$$\boxed{E[N(t)] = \text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i)** With $\lambda = 2$ bugs/day, $t = 1$ day, $n = 0$:
$$P\{N(1) = 0\} = \frac{(2 \cdot 1)^0 e^{-2}}{0!} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**(ii)** With $\lambda = 2$, $t = 3$ days, $\lambda t = 6$:
$$P\{N(3) \geq 5\} = 1 - P\{N(3) \leq 4\} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{4} \frac{6^k e^{-6}}{k!}$$

Compute each term:
- $k=0$: $e^{-6} \approx 0.00248$
- $k=1$: $6 e^{-6} \approx 0.01487$
- $k=2$: $18 e^{-6} \approx 0.04462$
- $k=3$: $36 e^{-6} \approx 0.08924$
- $k=4$: $54 e^{-6} \approx 0.13385$

Sum $\approx 0.28506$, so $P\{N(3) \geq 5\} \approx 1 - 0.2851 = 0.7149$.

**(iii)** Time to first bug $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(2)$. We need $P\{X_1 > 1\}$:
$$P\{X_1 > 1\} = e^{-2 \cdot 1} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In (ii), students often forget to compute $1 - P\{N(3) \leq 4\}$ and instead try to directly sum $k=5$ to $\infty$, which is *infinite*. Always use the **complement rule** when asked for "at least" probabilities. Loses 2 marks for this oversight.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Counting Process Definition:** A stochastic process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ that counts the cumulative number of events up to time $t$. Must satisfy $N(0)=0$, integer-valued, non-decreasing, and right-continuous.
- **Poisson Process:** The *only* counting process with **independent and stationary** increments where event counts follow the Poisson distribution $P\{N(t)=n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$.
- **Mean = Variance = $\lambda t$:** A signature property of the Poisson process. Use it to verify solutions and for sanity checks.
- **Inter-arrival Duality:** Poisson($\lambda$) process $\Leftrightarrow$ i.i.d. Exponential($\lambda$) inter-arrival times. This is the **iff** relationship that is the heart of KTU 14-mark problems.
- **Erlang / Gamma Connection:** $T_n = X_1 + \dots + X_n$ follows the Erlang distribution with PDF $\frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$.
- **Memoryless Property:** $P\{X > s + t \mid X > t\} = P\{X > s\}$ — only true for the exponential distribution, making it ideal for modeling "random" events.
- **Engineering Applications:** Use Poisson processes for modeling **arrivals** (packets, customers, phone calls, hardware failures, software bugs, radioactive decay).
- **Complement Rule:** For "at least $n$" probabilities, always use $P\{N(t) \geq n\} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} P\{N(t) = k\}$.
- **Stationarity vs Independence:** Independence applies to *disjoint* intervals; stationarity means the distribution depends only on interval *length*.
- **KTU Style Reminder:** Always state the axioms of a counting process explicitly before applying the Poisson PMF. Examiners award partial marks for clear definitions.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Counting Process

A **stochastic process** $\{N(t),\, t \geq 0\}$ is called a **counting process** if it represents the total number of events (or "arrivals") that have occurred in the time interval $[0,\, t]$. Formally, it must satisfy the following axiomatic properties used in KTU Board Examinations:

1. **Initial Condition:** $N(0) = 0$ (no events have occurred at time zero).
2. **Non-negativity:** $N(t) \geq 0$ for all $t \geq 0$.
3. **Integer-Valued State Space:** $N(t) \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ (the process can only take non-negative integer values).
4. **Monotonicity / Non-decreasing:** If $s < t$, then $N(s) \leq N(t)$ (events are cumulative and cannot be "un-counted").
5. **Right-Continuity:** The function $t \mapsto N(t)$ is right-continuous, i.e., $\lim_{s \to t^+} N(s) = N(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** A counting process is essentially a *step function* that jumps by **+1** at every event occurrence. The state space is always the set of non-negative integers $\mathbb{N}_0 = \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting at a **busy traffic intersection in Kochi** with a tally counter in your hand. Every time a car passes, you press the button on your counter.

- Your counter reading at time $t$ is exactly $N(t)$.
- $N(t)$ can only go **up** — once a car has passed, you cannot "un-pass" it (this gives us **monotonicity**).
- $N(t)$ must always be a **whole number** — you cannot have 4.7 cars pass.
- $N(0) = 0$ — at the start, no cars have passed.

This tally counter is the perfect real-world embodiment of a **counting process**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Counting processes are the mathematical backbone of **queueing theory**, **reliability engineering**, **network traffic modeling**, and **hardware failure analysis** — all of which are core areas in Computer Science and Information Technology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Properties of a Counting Process

Beyond the axiomatic definition, two *incremental properties* govern how a counting process evolves. These are heavily tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

### Property A: Independent Increments
A counting process has **independent increments** if the number of events occurring in **disjoint** time intervals are mutually independent random variables.

Mathematically, for any $0 = t_0 < t_1 < t_2 < \dots < t_n$, the random variables
$$N(t_1) - N(t_0),\; N(t_2) - N(t_1),\; \dots,\; N(t_n) - N(t_{n-1})$$
are **mutually independent**.

### Property B: Stationary Increments
A counting process has **stationary increments** if the distribution of the number of events in any interval depends *only* on the **length** of the interval, not on its starting point.

Mathematically, $N(t + s) - N(s)$ has the same distribution as $N(t) - N(0) = N(t)$ for all $s, t \geq 0$.

## 2.2 The Poisson Process — The King of Counting Processes

A counting process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is called a **Poisson Process** with rate $\lambda > 0$ if:

1. $N(0) = 0$.
2. It has **independent increments**.
3. The number of events in any interval of length $t$ follows a **Poisson distribution** with mean $\lambda t$:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trigger Word:** Whenever the problem says "events occur at a constant average rate $\lambda$", the answer is almost always a **Poisson Process**.

## 2.3 Inter-Arrival Times

Let $T_1, T_2, T_3, \dots$ be the time of the first, second, third, … event. Define the **inter-arrival times** as
$$X_n = T_n - T_{n-1}, \quad n \geq 1 \quad (\text{with } T_0 = 0).$$

For a **Poisson process with rate $\lambda$**:
- Each $X_n$ is **independent**.
- Each $X_n$ is **exponentially distributed** with parameter $\lambda$ and mean $1/\lambda$.
- Probability Density Function (PDF):
$$f_{X_n}(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$
- Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):
$$P\{X_n \leq x\} = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property of Exponential Distribution:** $P\{X > s + t \mid X > t\} = P\{X > s\}$. This is the *only* continuous distribution with this property, and it is the reason Poisson processes model "random" event arrivals so well.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Key Parameter** | **Engineering Use** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Poisson PMF | $P\{N(t)=n\} = \dfrac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$ | $n \in \mathbb{N}_0$ | Event count probability |
| 2 | Mean of Poisson Process | $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$ | rate $\lambda > 0$ | Expected arrivals |
| 3 | Variance of Poisson Process | $\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$ | — | Spread of arrivals |
| 4 | Exponential PDF (Inter-arrival) | $f_X(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Time-between-events |
| 5 | Exponential CDF | $F_X(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Reliability / survival |
| 6 | Survival Function | $P\{X > x\} = e^{-\lambda x}$ | $x \geq 0$ | Hazard analysis |
| 7 | Mean Inter-arrival Time | $E[X] = 1/\lambda$ | minutes/event | Throughput design |
| 8 | Sum of Exponentials (Gamma) | $f_{T_n}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$ | $t \geq 0$ | Time of $n$-th event |
| 9 | Hazard Rate | $r(t) = \lambda$ (constant) | $t \geq 0$ | Constant failure rate |
| 10 | Conditional Probability | $P\{N(t+s)=n+m \mid N(s)=n\} = P\{N(t)=m\}$ | stationary increments | Memoryless behavior |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Inter-Arrival Times to Poisson Process

**Goal:** Show that if the inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, \dots$ are i.i.d. **exponential with rate $\lambda$**, then the number of events $N(t)$ in $[0,\,t]$ is **Poisson with mean $\lambda t$**.

### Step 1 — Define the Event Count
The event $\{N(t) \geq n\}$ means that the $n$-th event has occurred at or before time $t$. That is,
$$\{N(t) \geq n\} = \{T_n \leq t\}$$
where $T_n = X_1 + X_2 + \dots + X_n$ is the sum of the first $n$ inter-arrival times (also called the **$n$-th order statistic** of the arrival times).

### Step 2 — Compute the CDF of $T_n$
Since $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, the sum $T_n$ follows a **Gamma (Erlang) distribution** with shape $n$ and rate $\lambda$. The PDF of $T_n$ is given by convolution:
$$f_{T_n}(t) = \int_0^t f_{X_{n}}(t - s) f_{T_{n-1}}(s)\, ds$$

By induction on $n$, we obtain:
$$f_{T_n}(t) = \frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}, \quad t \geq 0$$

### Step 3 — Derive the CDF via Integration
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \int_0^t \frac{\lambda^n s^{n-1} e^{-\lambda s}}{(n-1)!}\, ds$$

Performing a change of variable $u = \lambda s$, so $du = \lambda\, ds$:
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \int_0^{\lambda t} \frac{u^{n-1} e^{-u}}{(n-1)!}\, du$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Incomplete Gamma Function
This integral is the **regularized upper incomplete Gamma function** $1 - \Gamma(n, \lambda t)/(n-1)!$. By the **Poisson–Gamma relationship** (also called the *Poisson–Erlang duality*):
$$P\{T_n \leq t\} = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

### Step 5 — Invert the Relation to Get $P\{N(t) = n\}$
We know that
$$P\{N(t) \geq n\} = P\{T_n \leq t\}$$

Therefore,
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = P\{N(t) \geq n\} - P\{N(t) \geq n+1\}$$

This gives:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} - \sum_{k=n+1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

$$\boxed{P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$) **if and only if** the counting process is a **Poisson process with rate $\lambda$**. This is the single most important duality in KTU Board examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean and Variance of $N(t)$

### Mean
Using the fact that $E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P\{N(t) > n\}$ and $P\{N(t) > n\} = P\{T_{n+1} \leq t\}$:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P\{T_{n+1} \leq t\} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

Interchanging the order of summation:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!} \cdot k = \lambda t \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{k-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(k-1)!}$$

Letting $j = k-1$:
$$E[N(t)] = \lambda t \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^j e^{-\lambda t}}{j!} = \lambda t \cdot 1$$

$$\boxed{E[N(t)] = \lambda t}$$

### Variance
A similar (lengthier) computation using $\text{Var}[N(t)] = E[N(t)^2] - (E[N(t)])^2$ and the Taylor expansion of $e^{\lambda t}$ yields:
$$\boxed{\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *For a Poisson process, the mean always equals the variance, and both equal $\lambda t$.* This is a unique property — no other common counting process has this equality.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Packets arrive at a router according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = 3$ packets/second. Find:
1. The probability that exactly 2 packets arrive in the first second.
2. The expected number of packets in 5 seconds.
3. The probability that the time between consecutive packets is more than 0.5 seconds.

**Solution to (1):**
$$P\{N(1) = 2\} = \frac{(3 \cdot 1)^2 e^{-3 \cdot 1}}{2!} = \frac{9 e^{-3}}{2} \approx 0.2240$$

**Solution to (2):**
$$E[N(5)] = 3 \cdot 5 = 15 \text{ packets}$$

**Solution to (3):** The inter-arrival time $X \sim \text{Exp}(3)$. We need $P\{X > 0.5\}$:
$$P\{X > 0.5\} = e^{-3 \cdot 0.5} = e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a counting process. State the four axioms that $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ must satisfy to qualify as a counting process.**

**Model Answer:**
A **counting process** is a stochastic process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ that records the cumulative number of events that have occurred up to time $t$. The four axioms are:
1. $N(0) = 0$ — process starts at zero.
2. $N(t)$ is integer-valued and non-negative: $N(t) \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}$.
3. $N(t)$ is non-decreasing: if $s < t$, then $N(s) \leq N(t)$.
4. $N(t)$ is right-continuous: $\lim_{s \to t^+} N(s) = N(t)$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the relationship between the Poisson counting process and the exponential inter-arrival times.**

**Model Answer:**
If $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$, then the inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, X_3, \dots$ are **independent and identically distributed** random variables, each following an **exponential distribution** with parameter $\lambda$. Conversely, if the inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$), then the counting process is Poisson with rate $\lambda$. This is a **two-way equivalence (iff relationship)**.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**(a)** Customers arrive at a bank according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda = 4$ customers per hour. Compute:
- (i) The probability that exactly 3 customers arrive in the first 30 minutes. **[3 marks]**
- (ii) The expected number of customers in 2 hours and the variance. **[2 marks]**
- (iii) The probability that the time between two consecutive arrivals is between 10 and 20 minutes. **[2 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the Poisson PMF starting from the assumption that the inter-arrival times are i.i.d. exponential($\lambda$). Show all steps explicitly. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i)** Here $t = 0.5$ hours, $\lambda t = 4 \cdot 0.5 = 2$, $n = 3$.
$$P\{N(0.5) = 3\} = \frac{2^3 e^{-2}}{3!} = \frac{8 e^{-2}}{6} = \frac{4 e^{-2}}{3} \approx 0.1804$$

**(ii)** For $t = 2$ hours:
$$E[N(2)] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \text{ customers}, \quad \text{Var}[N(2)] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8$$

**(iii)** Inter-arrival $X \sim \text{Exp}(4)$ (rate per hour). We need $P\{1/6 \leq X \leq 1/3\}$:
$$P\{1/6 \leq X \leq 1/3\} = F(1/3) - F(1/6) = \left(1 - e^{-4/3}\right) - \left(1 - e^{-4/6}\right)$$
$$= e^{-2/3} - e^{-4/3} \approx 0.5134 - 0.2636 = 0.2498$$

#### Model Solution for (b): The Derivation

This is a complete re-derivation of the result. Follow the steps from Section 3.1 above:

1. Define inter-arrival times $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, i.i.d.
2. Define arrival times $T_n = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ (Erlang/Gamma distributed).
3. Use the equivalence $\{N(t) \geq n\} = \{T_n \leq t\}$.
4. Derive the Erlang CDF:
$$F_{T_n}(t) = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$
5. Use the telescoping difference $P\{N(t) = n\} = P\{N(t) \geq n\} - P\{N(t) \geq n+1\}$ to obtain the final result:
$$P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write $P\{T_n \leq t\}$ directly as $\frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$ — this is **WRONG**. $P\{T_n \leq t\}$ is the **CDF** of a Gamma distribution, which equals a *tail sum* of Poisson probabilities, not a single Poisson PMF. The final Poisson PMF is obtained by the **telescoping difference** of two such tail sums. Loses 2-3 marks if done incorrectly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**(a)** Define a **Poisson process** with rate $\lambda$. Prove that for a Poisson process, $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$ and $\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Software bugs are reported to a project manager at a constant average rate of 2 bugs per day, modeled as a Poisson process. Find:
- (i) The probability that no bugs are reported on a given day. **[2 marks]**
- (ii) The probability that at least 5 bugs are reported in 3 days. **[3 marks]**
- (iii) The probability that the time to the first bug exceeds 1 day. **[2 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** A counting process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$ if:
- $N(0) = 0$.
- It has independent increments.
- $N(t)$ is Poisson distributed: $P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$.

**Proof of Mean:** Using the definition of expectation for a Poisson random variable:
$$E[N(t)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!} = (\lambda t) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$$

Letting $m = n - 1$:
$$E[N(t)] = (\lambda t) \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^m e^{-\lambda t}}{m!} = (\lambda t) \cdot e^{\lambda t} e^{-\lambda t} = \lambda t$$

**Proof of Variance:** Using $E[N(t)^2] = E[N(t)(N(t)-1)] + E[N(t)]$:
$$E[N(t)(N(t)-1)] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n(n-1) \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!} = (\lambda t)^2 \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-2} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-2)!} = (\lambda t)^2$$

Therefore:
$$\text{Var}[N(t)] = E[N(t)^2] - (E[N(t)])^2 = \left[(\lambda t)^2 + \lambda t\right] - (\lambda t)^2 = \lambda t$$

$$\boxed{E[N(t)] = \text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t} \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i)** With $\lambda = 2$ bugs/day, $t = 1$ day, $n = 0$:
$$P\{N(1) = 0\} = \frac{(2 \cdot 1)^0 e^{-2}}{0!} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

**(ii)** With $\lambda = 2$, $t = 3$ days, $\lambda t = 6$:
$$P\{N(3) \geq 5\} = 1 - P\{N(3) \leq 4\} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{4} \frac{6^k e^{-6}}{k!}$$

Compute each term:
- $k=0$: $e^{-6} \approx 0.00248$
- $k=1$: $6 e^{-6} \approx 0.01487$
- $k=2$: $18 e^{-6} \approx 0.04462$
- $k=3$: $36 e^{-6} \approx 0.08924$
- $k=4$: $54 e^{-6} \approx 0.13385$

Sum $\approx 0.28506$, so $P\{N(3) \geq 5\} \approx 1 - 0.2851 = 0.7149$.

**(iii)** Time to first bug $X_1 \sim \text{Exp}(2)$. We need $P\{X_1 > 1\}$:
$$P\{X_1 > 1\} = e^{-2 \cdot 1} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In (ii), students often forget to compute $1 - P\{N(3) \leq 4\}$ and instead try to directly sum $k=5$ to $\infty$, which is *infinite*. Always use the **complement rule** when asked for "at least" probabilities. Loses 2 marks for this oversight.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Counting Process Definition:** A stochastic process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ that counts the cumulative number of events up to time $t$. Must satisfy $N(0)=0$, integer-valued, non-decreasing, and right-continuous.
- **Poisson Process:** The *only* counting process with **independent and stationary** increments where event counts follow the Poisson distribution $P\{N(t)=n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$.
- **Mean = Variance = $\lambda t$:** A signature property of the Poisson process. Use it to verify solutions and for sanity checks.
- **Inter-arrival Duality:** Poisson($\lambda$) process $\Leftrightarrow$ i.i.d. Exponential($\lambda$) inter-arrival times. This is the **iff** relationship that is the heart of KTU 14-mark problems.
- **Erlang / Gamma Connection:** $T_n = X_1 + \dots + X_n$ follows the Erlang distribution with PDF $\frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$.
- **Memoryless Property:** $P\{X > s + t \mid X > t\} = P\{X > s\}$ — only true for the exponential distribution, making it ideal for modeling "random" events.
- **Engineering Applications:** Use Poisson processes for modeling **arrivals** (packets, customers, phone calls, hardware failures, software bugs, radioactive decay).
- **Complement Rule:** For "at least $n$" probabilities, always use $P\{N(t) \geq n\} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} P\{N(t) = k\}$.
- **Stationarity vs Independence:** Independence applies to *disjoint* intervals; stationarity means the distribution depends only on interval *length*.
- **KTU Style Reminder:** Always state the axioms of a counting process explicitly before applying the Poisson PMF. Examiners award partial marks for clear definitions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

topics.title: The Poisson Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAMAT301 - MATHEMATICS FOR COMPUTER AND INFORMATION SCIENCE-3 > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Poisson Process # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Poisson Process A **counting process** $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is said to be a **Poisson process with rate $\lambda > 0$** if it satisfies the following four postulates: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Rigorously Tested)** > 1. **$N(0) = 0$** — The process starts with zero events at time zero. > 2. **Independent Increments** — The number of events occurring in disjoint time intervals are independent random variables. > 3. **Stationary Increments** — The distribution of the number of events in an interval depends only on the length of the interval, not on its location in time. > 4. **Poisson Distributed Counts** — For any $t > 0$, the random variable $N(t)$ follows a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda t$: > > $$P\{N(t) = n\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ The parameter $\lambda$ (measured in **events per unit time**) is called the **rate** or **intensity** of the process. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Café Counter > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: ☕ The Coffee Shop Queue** > Imagine you are sitting in a small café. Customers walk in **one at a time** at completely random moments — no schedule, no pattern. Let $N(t)$ be the total number of customers who have entered by time $t$. > > - The average rate of customer arrivals is **$\lambda$ customers per hour** (a fixed constant, say 5/hr). > - Whether a customer arrives in the next 10 minutes has **nothing to do** with what happened in the previous hour (independent increments). > - The chance of getting, say, 3 customers in 12 minutes is the same as 3 customers in any other 12-minute window (stationary increments). > > This random, memoryless, and orderly chaos is exactly what a **Poisson Process** models. ## 1.3 Why the Poisson Process Matters in Computer Science | Engineering Domain | Application of Poisson Process | |---|---| | **Network Traffic Engineering** | Modeling packet arrivals at a router, call arrivals at a switch | | **Queueing Theory (M/M/1, M/M/c)** | Inter-arrival times of customers in single/multi-server systems | | **Reliability Engineering** | Hardware failure arrivals, software bug discoveries | | **Cybersecurity** | Cyber-attack intrusions, DDoS packet bursts | | **Database Systems** | Transaction arrivals, lock requests per second | | **Wireless Communication** | Signal interference, channel access events | ## 1.4 Alternative Defining Postulates (Equivalent Form) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Alternative Definition (Common in Examination)** > A counting process $\{N(t), t \geq 0\}$ is a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$ if: > 1. $N(0) = 0$ > 2. Has **stationary and independent increments** > 3. For sufficiently small $h > 0$: > - $P\{N(h) = 1\} = \lambda h + o(h)$ > - $P\{N(h) \geq 2\} = o(h)$ > > where $o(h)$ is a function such that $\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{o(h)}{h} = 0$. This second formulation is the **infinitesimal-rate definition** and is the standard test setup for KTU derivations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Four Postulates Explained > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Each Postulate Exists — The Engineering Rationale** - **Postulate 1: $N(0) = 0$** - Logical starting point. At the moment we begin observation, no events have occurred yet. This anchors the process to a well-defined origin. - **Postulate 2: Independent Increments** - *How it works:* For any $0 \leq t_1 < t_2 < t_3 < t_4$, the increments $N(t_2) - N(t_1)$ and $N(t_4) - N(t_3)$ are statistically independent. - *Why it matters:* It allows us to "decouple" the past from the future. The future evolution depends only on the present state, not on how we got there. - **Postulate 3: Stationary Increments** - *How it works:* The distribution of $N(t+s) - N(t)$ depends only on the length $s$, not on the starting point $t$. - *Why it matters:* The "physics" of the system doesn't change over time — there is no trend, no seasonality, no drift. - **Postulate 4: Poisson Marginal Distribution** - *How it works:* At every snapshot time $t$, the count $N(t)$ is Poisson distributed with mean $\lambda t$ and variance $\lambda t$. - *Why it matters:* Variance = Mean is a signature property of the Poisson distribution, often used as a goodness-of-fit check. ## 2.2 Mean and Variance of the Poisson Process Since $N(t) \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda t)$: $$E[N(t)] = \lambda t$$ $$\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The **Mean = Variance** equality is a critical check. If you simulate an arrival process and find $E[N(t)] \neq \text{Var}[N(t)]$, your process is **NOT Poisson** — it may be over-dispersed (compound Poisson) or under-dispersed. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Table — All Formulas Testable in KTU ESE** | \# | Property | Formula | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | PMF of $N(t)$ | $P\{N(t)=n\} = \dfrac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$ | Core defining equation | | 2 | Mean | $E[N(t)] = \lambda t$ | First moment | | 3 | Variance | $\text{Var}[N(t)] = \lambda t$ | Second central moment | | 4 | MGF | $M_{N(t)}(s) = e^{\lambda t (e^s - 1)}$ | Useful for sums | | 5 | First arrival time $T_1$ | $F_{T_1}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential($\lambda$) | | 6 | $n$-th arrival time $T_n$ PDF | $f_{T_n}(t) = \dfrac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$ | Erlang/Gamma$(n,\lambda)$ | | 7 | Inter-arrival time mean | $E[T_i] = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Constant for all $i$ | | 8 | Inter-arrival time variance | $\text{Var}(T_i) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | Constant for all $i$ | | 9 | Memoryless property | $P(T_1 > s+t \,\vert\, T_1 > s) = P(T_1 > t)$ | Exponential signature | | 10 | Order statistics density | $f_{T_{(k)}}(t) = \dfrac{n!}{(k-1)!(n-k)!} \left[1-e^{-\lambda t}\right]^{k-1} \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \left[e^{-\lambda t}\right]^{n-k}$ | $k$-th arrival among $n$ | | 11 | Conditional joint density | $f(t_1,\ldots,t_n \,\vert\, N(t)=n) = \dfrac{n!}{t^n}$ | Uniform on simplex | | 12 | Compound Poisson | $S(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N(t)} Y_i$ | Sum of iid random amounts | | 13 | MGF of $S(t)$ | $M_{S(t)}(s) = e^{\lambda t [M_Y(s) - 1]}$ | Where $M_Y$ is MGF of $Y_i$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Network Design**: If packets arrive according to a Poisson process at rate $\lambda = 1000$/sec, then the probability of getting exactly 1024 packets in one second is: $$P\{N(1) = 1024\} = \frac{(1000)^{1024} e^{-1000}}{1024!}$$ Computed via the normal approximation using Stirling's formula. - **Quality Control**: Number of defects per unit length in a cable often follows a Poisson process. If $\lambda = 0.5$ defects/km, then $E[N(10)] = 5$ defects per 10 km cable. - **Insurance Risk**: Aggregate claim amount $S(t)$ = sum of individual claim sizes up to time $t$, modeled as Compound Poisson. ## 2.5 Inter-Arrival Times: The Exponential Connection Let $T_1$ be the time of the **first event**, $T_2$ the time of the **second event**, and so on. The differences $X_i = T_i - T_{i-1}$ (with $T_0 = 0$) are called **inter-arrival times**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Theorem (Exam Favorite)** > The inter-arrival times $X_1, X_2, X_3, \ldots$ of a Poisson process are **independent and identically distributed (iid)** random variables, each following an **Exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$**. > > $$f_{X_i}(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \quad x \geq 0$$ > > This is a **two-way street**: a renewal process with iid Exponential inter-arrival times IS a Poisson process. ## 2.6 Memoryless Property (A Defining Hallmark) > [!NOTE] > **Memoryless Property of the Exponential Distribution** > > $$P(X > s+t \,\vert\, X > s) = P(X > t), \quad s, t \geq 0$$ > > **Engineering Interpretation:** If no event has occurred in $s$ units of time, the distribution of the *remaining waiting time* is exactly as if we had just started. The process has **no memory of the past**. ## 2.7 Distribution of $T_n$ (The $n$-th Arrival Time) Since $T_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i$ where $X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ are iid, by convolution: $$f_{T_n}(t) = \frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}, \quad t \geq 0$$ This is the **Erlang distribution** (or Gamma distribution with integer shape parameter $n$). $$E[T_n] = \frac{n}{\lambda}, \quad \text{Var}(T_n) = \frac{n}{\lambda^2}$$ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: PMF of $N(t)$ from the Infinitesimal Postulates **Setup:** We use the alternative postulates. For small $h > 0$: - $P\{N(h) = 0\} = 1 - \lambda h + o(h)$ - $P\{N(h) = 1\} = \lambda h + o(h)$ - $P\{N(h) \geq 2\} = o(h)$ Define $P_n(t) = P\{N(t) = n\}$. We derive a differential equation for $P_n(t)$. **Step 1: Condition on $N(t)$ at a small interval $h$.** By the law of total probability and independent increments: $$P_n(t+h) = P\{N(t+h) = n\}$$ **Step 2: Break into cases based on what happened in the first $h$ time units.** $$P_n(t+h) = P_n(t) \cdot P\{N(h) = 0\} + P_{n-1}(t) \cdot P\{N(h) = 1\} + \sum_{k=2}^{n} P_{n-k}(t) \cdot P\{N(h) = k\}$$ **Step 3: Substitute the postulates.** $$P_n(t+h) = P_n(t)(1 - \lambda h + o(h)) + P_{n-1}(t)(\lambda h + o(h)) + o(h)$$ **Step 4: Rearrange.** $$P_n(t+h) - P_n(t) = -\lambda h P_n(t) + \lambda h P_{n-1}(t) + o(h)$$ **Step 5: Divide by $h$ and take the limit $h \to 0$.** $$\frac{dP_n(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_n(t) + \lambda P_{n-1}(t), \quad n \geq 1$$ **Step 6: Boundary case $n = 0$:** $$P_0(t+h) = P_0(t)(1 - \lambda h + o(h)) \implies \frac{dP_0(t)}{dt} = -\lambda P_0(t)$$ **Step 7: Solve the boundary ODE.** $$\frac{dP_0}{P_0} = -\lambda dt \implies \ln P_0(t) = -\lambda t + C$$ Using $P_0(0) = 1$, we get $C = 0$: $$P_0(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 8: Solve recursively for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$** Assume $P_{n-1}(t) = \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$. The ODE for $P_n$ is: $$\frac{dP_n}{dt} + \lambda P_n = \lambda \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^{n-1} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!}$$ **Step 9: Solve using integrating factor $e^{\lambda t}$.** $$\frac{d}{dt}\left[e^{\lambda t} P_n(t)\right] = \frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1} e^{\lambda t} e^{-\lambda t}}{(n-1)!} = \frac{\lambda^n t^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}$$ **Step 10: Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.** $$e^{\lambda t} P_n(t) - P_n(0) = \frac{\lambda^n t^n}{n! \cdot (n-1)!} \cdot (n-1)! = \frac{\lambda^n t^n}{n!}$$ **Step 11: Since $P_n(0) = 0$ for $n \geq 1$:** $$P_n(t) = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$$ $$\boxed{P_n(t) = \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots}$$ This is the **Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda t$**. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Distribution of $T_1$ (First Arrival Time) **Goal:** Find $F_{T_1}(t) = P\{T_1 \leq t\}$. **Step 1: Realize that $T_1 > t$ means no events in $(0, t]$.** $$P\{T_1 > t\} = P\{N(t) = 0\}$$ **Step 2: Use the Poisson PMF with $n = 0$.** $$P\{T_1 > t\} = \frac{(\lambda t)^0 e^{-\lambda t}}{0!} = e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 3: Therefore, the CDF is:** $$F_{T_1}(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0$$ **Step 4: Differentiate to get the PDF.** $$f_{T_1}(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\left[1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right] = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0$$ This confirms $T_1 \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Conditional Distribution of Arrival Times Given $N(t) = n$ **Theorem (Uniform Order Statistics):** Given that exactly $n$ events have occurred in $(0, t]$, the unordered arrival times $(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ are iid **Uniform$(0, t)$** random variables. **Proof Sketch:** **Step 1: Joint density of $T_1, \ldots, T_n$ unconditional on $N(t)$.** For $0 < t_1 < t_2 < \cdots < t_n < t$: $$f(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n) = f_{X_1}(t_1) f_{X_2}(t_2 - t_1) \cdots f_{X_n}(t_n - t_{n-1})$$ **Step 2: Substitute Exponential PDFs.** $$f(t_1, \ldots, t_n) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t_1} \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda(t_2 - t_1)} \cdots \lambda e^{-\lambda(t_n - t_{n-1})}$$ **Step 3: Telescope the exponentials.** $$f(t_1, \ldots, t_n) = \lambda^n e^{-\lambda t_n}$$ **Step 4: Apply conditional probability formula.** $$f(t_1, \ldots, t_n \mid N(t) = n) = \frac{f(t_1, \ldots, t_n)}{P\{N(t) = n\}}$$ **Step 5: Substitute the Poisson PMF.** $$f(t_1, \ldots, t_n \mid N(t) = n) = \frac{\lambda^n e^{-\lambda t_n}}{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t} / n!} = \frac{n!}{t^n}$$ **Step 6: Conclusion — Uniform on the simplex.** $$\boxed{f(t_1, \ldots, t_n \mid N(t) = n) = \frac{n!}{t^n}, \quad 0 < t_1 < t_2 < \cdots < t_n < t}$$ This is the **joint density of the order statistics of $n$ iid Uniform$(0,t)$ random variables**. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Consequence:** Conditional on $N(t) = n$, the $n$ arrival times have the same joint distribution as the order statistics of $n$ iid Uniform$(0,t)$ variables. This is a classic KTU question. ## 3.4 Derivation: MGF of $N(t)$ and Sum Property **Step 1: MGF definition.** $$M_{N(t)}(s) = E[e^{s N(t)}] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} e^{sn} \cdot \frac{(\lambda t)^n e^{-\lambda t}}{n!}$$ **Step 2: Factor constants.** $$M_{N(t)}(s) = e^{-\lambda t} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\lambda t e^s)^n}{n!}$$ **Step 3: Recognize the Taylor series of $e^x$.** $$M_{N(t)}(s) = e^{-\lambda t} \cdot e^{\lambda t e^s} = e^{\lambda t (e^s - 1)}$$ **Step 4: Differentiate to find mean and variance.** $$E[N(t)] = M'_{N(t)}(0) = \lambda t e^s \cdot e^{\lambda t(e^s-1)} \Big|_{s=0} = \lambda t$$ $$\text{Var}[N(t)] = M''_{N(t)}(0) - [M'_{N(t)}(0)]^2 = \lambda t$$ Both confirm earlier results. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.5 Derivation: Compound Poisson Process — Mean and Variance **Setup:** $S(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N(t)} Y_i$ where $Y_i$ are iid with $E[Y_i] = \mu_Y$ and $\text{Var}(Y_i) = \sigma_Y^2$, independent of $N(t)$. **Step 1: Condition on $N(t)$ to find $E[S(t)]$.** $$E[S(t)] = E[E[S(t) \mid N(t)]] = E[N(t) \cdot \mu_Y] = \mu_Y \cdot \lambda t$$ **Step 2: Use the law of total variance.** $$\text{Var}[S(t)] = E[\text{Var}(S(t) \mid N(t))] + \text{Var}[E[S(t) \mid N(t)]]$$ **Step 3: Substitute inner expressions.** $$\text{Var}[S(t)] = E[N(t) \sigma_Y^2] + \text{Var}[N(t) \mu_Y] = \sigma_Y^2 \lambda t + \mu_Y^2 \lambda t$$ $$\boxed{E[S(t)] = \lambda t \mu_Y, \quad \text{Var}[S(t)] = \lambda t (\sigma_Y^2 + \mu_Y^2)}$$ This is widely used in **actuarial science** (aggregate claims) and **reliability engineering** (total cost of failures). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture of the Poisson Process: Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef defBlock fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#4a90e2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef mathBlock fill:#2d5a3d,stroke:#50c878,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef appBlock fill:#5a3d2d,stroke:#c87850,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef resBlock fill:#4a2d5a,stroke:#9050c8,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff A["AXIOM LAYER
Postulates of the Process"]:::defBlock B["PROBABILITY LAYER
Distributions and Moments"]:::mathBlock C["TIME LAYER
Inter Arrival and Arrival Times"]:::mathBlock D["EXTENSION LAYER
Compound and Non Homogeneous"]:::mathBlock E["APPLICATION LAYER
Engineering Domains"]:::appBlock A1["N of zero equals zero"]:::defBlock A2["Independent Increments"]:::defBlock A3["Stationary Increments"]:::defBlock A4["Poisson Marginal Distribution"]:::defBlock A --> A1 A --> A2 A --> A3 A --> A4 B1["P of N of t equals n
equals lambda t to n times e to minus lambda t divided by n factorial"]:::mathBlock B2["Mean equals lambda times t"]:::mathBlock B3["Variance equals lambda times t"]:::mathBlock B4["MGF equals e to the power lambda t times e to s minus 1"]:::mathBlock A4 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B1 --> B4 C1["Inter Arrival Times X sub i
are iid Exponential lambda"]:::mathBlock C2["Nth Arrival Time T sub n
follows Erlang distribution"]:::mathBlock C3["Memoryless Property
P of X greater than s plus t given X greater than s equals P of X greater than t"]:::mathBlock C4["Conditional Uniform Order Statistics
Given N of t equals n, arrivals are
Uniform on the simplex"]:::mathBlock C1 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C1 --> C4 D1["Compound Poisson Process
S of t equals sum of Y sub i"]:::appBlock D2["Non Homogeneous Poisson Process
Rate lambda of t depends on time"]:::appBlock D3["Poisson Process Splitting and Merging Theorems"]:::appBlock C1 --> D1 A --> D2 A --> D3 E1["Network Traffic Engineering"]:::appBlock E2["Queueing Theory Models M M 1 and M M c"]:::appBlock E3["Reliability and Failure Analysis"]:::appBlock E4["Cybersecurity Intrusion Detection"]:::appBlock D1 --> E1 D1 --> E2 D3 --> E3 D3 --> E4 ``` ## 4.2 Time Evolution of a Sample Path ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef timeNode fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#4a90e2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef eventNode fill:#c83232,stroke:#ff6b6b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef stateNode fill:#2d5a3d,stroke:#50c878,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff t0["T EQUALS 0
N equals 0 events"]:::timeNode e1["Event 1 Arrives
At time T1"]:::eventNode s1["N increases to 1
Step Jump of size 1"]:::stateNode t1["T EQUALS 0.5
N equals 1 event"]:::timeNode e2["Event 2 Arrives

N equals 2 events"]:::timeNode e3["Event 3 Arrives
