# 🧪 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`  
Define a **recurrent state** in a Markov chain. How is it distinguished from a **transient state**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
A state $i$ of a Markov chain is called *recurrent* if, starting from $i$, the probability of returning to $i$ at some future time is 1, i.e. $f_{ii} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_{ii}^{(n)} = 1$. A state is *transient* if $f_{ii} < 1$, meaning there is a strictly positive probability of *never* returning. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the contrast, 1 mark for the correct use of the symbol $f_{ii}$.)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`  
What is the **mean recurrence time** $\mu_i$? When is a recurrent state called *null recurrent*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The mean recurrence time is $\mu_i = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\, f_{ii}^{(n)}$ — the expected step at which the chain first returns to $i$. A recurrent state is called *null recurrent* if $\mu_i = \infty$ (the mean return time diverges), even though the chain returns with probability 1. (1 mark each for definition, formula, and null-recurrent condition.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Marks (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Note for KTU 2024 Scheme:** Answer *one* of the two questions. Each carries **14 marks**, split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Show *all* steps; partial credit is awarded for correct intermediate reasoning.

---

### ✅ Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define *recurrent* and *transient* states. State and prove the theorem that a state $i$ is recurrent if and only if $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ii}^{(n)} = \infty$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Definition (1 mark).* State $i$ is **recurrent** if $f_{ii} = 1$, **transient** if $f_{ii} < 1$, where $f_{ii} = \sum_{n\ge 1} f_{ii}^{(n)}$.

*Step 2 — Renewal identity (2 marks).* Condition on the time $k$ of the first return:

$$p_{ii}^{(n)} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} f_{ii}^{(k)}\, p_{ii}^{(n-k)}, \qquad n \ge 1$$

*Step 3 — Generating functions (1 mark).* Let $P(z) = \sum p_{ii}^{(n)} z^{n}$ and $F(z) = \sum f_{ii}^{(n)} z^{n}$. Multiplying the renewal identity by $z^{n}$ and summing:

$$P(z) - 1 \;=\; F(z)\, P(z) \;\Longrightarrow\; P(z) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - F(z)}$$

*Step 4 — Abel limit (2 marks).* Letting $z \to 1^{-}$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ii}^{(n)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - f_{ii}}$$

*Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark).* Hence $f_{ii}=1 \iff \sum p_{ii}^{(n)} = \infty$, proving the theorem. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** For the Markov chain with transition matrix

$$P \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\[2pt] 1/2 & 0 & 1/2 \\[2pt] 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

classify each of the three states as recurrent or transient, and determine the mean recurrence time where applicable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Compute the relevant $n$-step return probabilities (3 marks).*

$$p_{11}^{(1)} = 0,\quad p_{11}^{(2)} = (P^2)_{11} = 1/2,\quad p_{11}^{(3)} = 0,\quad p_{11}^{(4)} = 1/2,\dots$$

In fact, for $n \ge 1$:

$$p_{11}^{(n)} \;=\; \begin{cases} 1/2 & n \text{ even} \\ 0 & n \text{ odd} \end{cases}$$

*Step 2 — Apply the criterion (2 marks).*

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{11}^{(n)} \;=\; 1 + 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} + 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} + 0 + \cdots \;=\; \infty$$

So state 1 is **recurrent**.

*Step 3 — Classify the other states (1 mark).* All three states communicate (1↔2↔3↔2↔1), forming a single closed class, so **states 0 and 2 are also recurrent** by the communication-class rule.

*Step 4 — Mean recurrence times (1 mark).* By symmetry of the chain, $\pi_1 = \pi_2 = \pi_3 = 1/3$ is the stationary distribution (it satisfies $\pi P = \pi$). Hence

$$\mu_1 \;=\; \mu_2 \;=\; \mu_3 \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi_i} \;=\; 3$$

All three are **positive recurrent**.

---

### ✅ Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the *expected number of visits* to a state $i$ starting from $i$. Use it to prove that a state is recurrent iff the expected number of visits is infinite. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Setup (1 mark).* Let $N_i = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{1}_{\{X_n = i\}}$ be the total number of visits (including time 0). Then

$$E[N_i \mid X_0 = i] \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P(X_n = i \mid X_0 = i) \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ii}^{(n)}$$

*Step 2 — Decomposition by first-return time (3 marks).* Condition on the *first* return occurring at time $k \ge 1$, after which the chain "restarts" from $i$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ii}^{(n)} \;=\; 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} f_{ii}^{(k)} \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} p_{ii}^{(m)}$$

The factor 1 accounts for the starting visit at time 0.

*Step 3 — Solve (1 mark).* Let $S = \sum p_{ii}^{(n)}$. Then

$$S \;=\; 1 + f_{ii}\, S \;\Longrightarrow\; S (1 - f_{ii}) \;=\; 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; S \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - f_{ii}}$$

*Step 4 — Dichotomy (2 marks).* $f_{ii} = 1 \Rightarrow S = \infty$ (recurrent). $f_{ii} < 1 \Rightarrow S = 1/(1-f_{ii}) < \infty$ (transient). Hence $S = \infty \iff$ state $i$ is recurrent. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** A two-state Markov chain has transition matrix

$$P \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1-p & p \\[2pt] q & 1-q \end{pmatrix}, \qquad 0 < p,q < 1$$

Show that *both states are recurrent* if and only if $p = q = 0$ or $p = q = 1$. Otherwise classify each state. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Compute $p_{11}^{(n)}$ (2 marks).* By induction, the $(1,1)$-entry of $P^n$ is

$$p_{11}^{(n)} \;=\; \frac{q}{p+q} + \frac{p}{p+q}\,(1-p-q)^{n}$$

(verify for $n=0$ and $n=1$, then use the recurrence $P^{n+1}=P\cdot P^{n}$).

*Step 2 — Sum over $n$ (2 marks).* Provided $p + q \neq 0$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{11}^{(n)} \;=\; \begin{cases} \infty & \text{if } \vert 1-p-q \vert = 1 \text{ i.e. } pq=0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{1-(1-p-q)} = \dfrac{1}{p+q} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Step 3 — Apply the criterion (2 marks).*
- If $p=q=0$: $P=I$, both states are absorbing → **recurrent** ($\mu_i=1$).
- If $p=q=1$: chain alternates deterministically → **recurrent** ($\mu_i=2$).
- If $0 < p+q \le 1$ and $pq \neq 0$: $\sum p_{11}^{(n)} = 1/(p+q) < \infty$ → **transient** (and by symmetry, state 2 is also transient with the same expected-visit count).

*Step 4 — Verification by communication class (1 mark).* In all cases $\{1,2\}$ is a single communicating class; in the transient case, even though both states belong to the same class, the class is *not* closed when neither $p$ nor $q$ is zero — but here the class *is* closed (no exit). However, the algebra above is decisive.

> **Refined note (carries 1 mark):** With $0<p,q<1$ the only way $\sum p_{ii}^{(n)}<\infty$ is when $\vert 1-p-q \vert < 1$, which forces $0<p+q<2$. Both states are therefore **transient for $0 < p,q < 1$**, in agreement with the algebraic sum.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where students typically lose marks on Recurrent-State problems**  
> 1. **Forgetting the $n=0$ term** — $p_{ii}^{(0)}=1$ *must* be included when summing $\sum_{n\ge 0} p_{ii}^{(n)}$. Missing it changes the answer. (Lose ~1 mark.)  
> 2. **Confusing "recurrent" with "positive recurrent".** A recurrent state can still be *null* recurrent (infinite chains only). Always check finiteness of $\mu_i$. (Lose ~2 marks.)  
> 3. **Using the recurrence criterion on an infinite chain without verifying communication classes first.** Two communicating states share the same type — use it to deduce fast, but state it explicitly. (Lose ~1 mark.)  
> 4. **Mixing up absorbing and recurrent.** An absorbing state is recurrent, but *not every* recurrent state is absorbing. (Lose ~1 mark.)  
> 5. **Generating-function derivation:** Skipping the Abel-limit step loses full credit on 7-mark derivation questions. (Lose ~2 marks.)  
> 6. **Not specifying "starting from $i$"** when computing $f_{ii}$. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous language.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Bullets — Recurrent State (Module 4)**  
> • $f_{ii}^{(n)} = P(\text{first return at }n) = P(X_n=i, X_k\neq i\ \forall\, 1\le k<n \mid X_0=i)$.  
> • $f_{ii} = \sum f_{ii}^{(n)}$. State is **recurrent iff $f_{ii}=1$**.  
> • $\mu_i = \sum n f_{ii}^{(n)}$. Finite $\Rightarrow$ positive; infinite $\Rightarrow$ null.  
> • **Recurrence criterion:** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ii}^{(n)} = \infty \iff i$ is recurrent.  
> • **Expected visits:** $E[N_i \mid X_0=i] = 1/(1-f_{ii})$.  
> • **Renewal identity:** $p_{ii}^{(n)} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} f_{ii}^{(k)} p_{ii}^{(n-k)}$.  
> • **Generating-function identity:** $P(z) = 1/(1-F(z))$.  
> • Two communicating states are both recurrent *or* both transient.  
> • In a *finite* chain, all states are recurrent; absorbing states are positive with $\mu=1$.  
> • In a *finite* chain, non-closed classes contain transient states.  
> • In an *infinite* chain, null recurrence is possible (e.g. symmetric simple random walk on $\mathbb{Z}$).  
> • Stationary probability for positive-recurrent state: $\pi_i = 1/\mu_i$.  
> • **Most-tested formula on KTU papers:** the recurrence criterion and the renewal/GF derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transient State
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GAMAT301 - MATHEMATICS FOR COMPUTER AND INFORMATION SCIENCE-3 > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transient State # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the rigorous theory of **Markov Chains** (a cornerstone topic of Module 4 in GAMAT301), every state $i$ in the state space $S$ is classified based on the long-run **return behaviour** of the chain when starting from that state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Transient State).** A state $i \in S$ of a discrete-time Markov chain $\{X_n\}$ is called a **transient state** if, starting from $i$, the probability that the chain will *ever* return to $i$ is **strictly less than 1**. Equivalently, the probability of *never* returning is **strictly positive**. Mathematically, a state $i$ is transient if and only if $$ f_{ii} \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_{ii}^{(n)} \;<\; 1 $$ where $f_{ii}^{(n)} = P(X_n = i,\, X_k \neq i \text{ for } 1 \le k < n \mid X_0 = i)$ is the *first-return probability in exactly $n$ steps*, and $f_{ii}$ is the *total probability of ever returning* to $i$. > [!NOTE] > **Companion Definition (Recurrent State).** A state $i$ is called **recurrent** (or **persistent**) if $f_{ii} = 1$. For a recurrent state, the chain returns to $i$ with probability 1, infinitely often almost surely. A further sub-classification into **positive recurrent** ($\mu_i < \infty$) and **null recurrent** ($\mu_i = \infty$) states is made based on the **mean recurrence time** $\mu_i = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\,f_{ii}^{(n)}$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition > [!TIP] > **The Tourist Analogy.** Imagine a tourist visiting three landmarks in a city: a coffee shop (State 1), a bookstore (State 2), and their hotel (State 3). > - The **hotel** (State 3) is *recurrent*: every evening, the tourist *will* return to it, almost surely, infinitely often. > - The **coffee shop** and **bookstore** (States 1 and 2) are *transient*: the tourist *might* stop by once or twice, but there is a positive probability that after leaving, they will *never come back*. > - Once a transient landmark is "abandoned", the chain has moved permanently to the recurrent (hotel) world. A transient state is therefore a **"one-way exit point"** of the chain: the chain *can* revisit it, but with positive probability it never does. Over the long run, the number of visits to a transient state is a **finite random variable** with probability 1. > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Interpretation.** In a computer network, a *failed node* is often modelled as a transient state — once the system leaves the failure state (the node recovers), there is a chance it will never fail again within the observation window. In queuing systems, transient states model the *warm-up phase* before the system reaches statistical equilibrium (steady state). ## 1.3 Why the Distinction Matters (KTU 2024 Outcome) According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO4)**, students must be able to *classify states of a Markov chain and analyse their limiting behaviour*. The transient/recurrent dichotomy is the **gateway** to: - **Steady-state (limiting) distribution analysis** — only recurrent states participate. - **Absorption probability problems** — transient states are the *starting points* whose trajectories we wish to track until absorption. - **Algorithmic applications** — Google's PageRank, MCMC samplers, and reliability models all rely on correct transient-state classification. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Classification Logic The classification of a state $i$ is determined entirely by the *return probability* $f_{ii}$. The logical hierarchy is: 1. **Step 1 — Compute the one-step return probability** $p_{ii}$ (the $(i,i)$-th entry of the transition matrix $P$). 2. **Step 2 — Derive the $n$-step first-return probabilities** $f_{ii}^{(n)}$ via the recurrence $$ f_{ii}^{(n)} \;=\; \sum_{k \neq i} p_{ik}^{(n-1)} \, p_{ki} $$ *Reason:* to *first-return* in $n$ steps, the chain must be in some $k \neq i$ at step $n-1$, and then move to $i$ at step $n$. 3. **Step 3 — Sum to infinity** to obtain $f_{ii} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_{ii}^{(n)}$. 4. **Step 4 — Apply the decision rule:** $$ \text{State } i \text{ is} \begin{cases} \text{recurrent (persistent)} & \text{if } f_{ii} = 1 \\[4pt] \text{transient} & \text{if } f_{ii} < 1 \end{cases} $$ 5. **Step 5 (For recurrent states only) — Sub-classify** by computing the **mean recurrence time** $$ \mu_i \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n \, f_{ii}^{(n)} $$ Then $i$ is **positive recurrent** if $\mu_i < \infty$, and **null recurrent** if $\mu_i = \infty$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Theorem (Finite State Space).** In a Markov chain with a *finite* state space, *every* recurrent state is positive recurrent. Null recurrence is a phenomenon that can occur **only in infinite (countable) state spaces**. This is a **high-frequency KTU question** in Part A. ## 2.2 Expected Number of Visits to a Transient State Let $V_i$ be the total number of visits to state $i$ (including the initial visit at time $0$). Then $$ E[V_i \mid X_0 = j] \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ji}^{(n)} $$ A state $i$ is **transient** if and only if this expected count is **finite** for every starting state $j$ — geometrically, because the probability of returning decays geometrically. > [!TIP] > **Intuition (Geometric Series Test).** If $f_{ii} < 1$, the chain attempts to return infinitely often, but each "attempt" is an independent Bernoulli trial with success probability $f_{ii}$. The number of returns is therefore a Geometric random variable with mean $f_{ii} / (1 - f_{ii})$, which is finite. ## 2.3 Fundamental Matrix & Absorption Analysis When a Markov chain possesses *transient* states and *recurrent* (especially absorbing) states, partition the transition matrix as $$ P \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} Q & R \\ \mathbf{0} & I \end{pmatrix} $$ where: - $Q$ is the $t \times t$ sub-matrix of one-step transitions **among the $t$ transient states**, - $R$ is the $t \times s$ sub-matrix of transitions **from transient to recurrent/recurrent** states, - $I$ is the identity on the recurrent block (closed irreducible classes). > [!NOTE] > **The Fundamental Matrix.** The matrix $$ N \;=\; (I - Q)^{-1} \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} Q^{n} $$ is called the **fundamental matrix**. Its entries have a beautiful probabilistic meaning: $$ (N)_{ij} \;=\; E\bigl[\text{number of visits to transient state } j \mid \text{started in transient } i\bigr] $$ > [!NOTE] > **The Absorption-Probability Matrix.** The matrix $$ B \;=\; N \, R $$ gives the **probability that the chain, starting in transient state $i$, is eventually absorbed in (or visits) recurrent state $j$** — assuming the chain is started in state $i$. Each row of $B$ sums to 1 when the recurrent classes are absorbing. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the definitive KTU 2024 cheat-sheet for transient-state analysis. Memorise every entry.** | **Symbol** | **Mathematical Definition** | **Probabilistic Meaning** | |---|---|---| | $p_{ij}^{(n)}$ | $(P^n)_{ij}$ | Probability of moving from $i$ to $j$ in exactly $n$ steps | | $f_{ij}^{(n)}$ | $P(X_n = j,\; X_k \neq j,\, 1 \le k < n \mid X_0 = i)$ | First-passage probability from $i$ to $j$ in exactly $n$ steps | | $f_{ij}$ | $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_{ij}^{(n)}$ | Probability of ever reaching $j$ from $i$ | | $f_{ii}$ | $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_{ii}^{(n)}$ | Return probability to $i$ (decides transient vs recurrent) | | $\mu_i$ | $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n\, f_{ii}^{(n)}$ | Mean recurrence time for state $i$ | | $E[V_j \mid X_0 = i]$ | $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ij}^{(n)}$ | Expected number of visits to $j$ starting from $i$ | | $Q$ | Sub-matrix on transient states | One-step transient-to-transient transitions | | $R$ | Sub-matrix transient $\to$ recurrent | One-step transient-to-recurrent transitions | | $N$ | $(I - Q)^{-1}$ | Fundamental matrix: expected visits | | $B$ | $N \cdot R$ | Matrix of absorption (or hitting) probabilities | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Where transient states appear in Computer Science & Engineering:** > - **PageRank (Google Search):** "Dangling" web pages with no outbound links are modelled as transient states; the algorithm modifies $P$ to make the chain ergodic and reach a unique stationary distribution. > - **Software Reliability:** A "bug-active" state is transient; once fixed, the system enters the recurrent "bug-free" operating state. The fundamental matrix $N$ gives the *expected number of bug-reactivations*. > - **Network Protocols:** Transient states model *retransmissions* in TCP — the system may loop through the transient block several times before settling into the "ACK received" absorbing state. > - **MCMC & Bayesian Inference:** Burn-in periods correspond to the chain escaping its transient region and entering the stationary distribution of the target. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Return Probability Recurrence We will prove the foundational identity $$ f_{ii}^{(n)} \;=\; \sum_{k \neq i} p_{ik}^{(n-1)} \, p_{ki} $$ **Starting Point.** By definition, $$ f_{ii}^{(n)} \;=\; P(X_n = i,\; X_1 \neq i,\; X_2 \neq i,\;\dots,\; X_{n-1} \neq i \mid X_0 = i) $$ **Step 1 — Condition on the state at time $n-1$.** Let $X_{n-1} = k$. By the law of total probability, $$ f_{ii}^{(n)} \;=\; \sum_{k \in S} P(X_{n-1} = k,\; X_1 \neq i,\;\dots,\;X_{n-2} \neq i \mid X_0 = i) \cdot P(X_n = i \mid X_{n-1} = k) $$ **Step 2 — Use the Markov property.** The term $P(X_n = i \mid X_{n-1} = k)$ depends only on $k$ and equals $p_{ki}$. **Step 3 — Enforce the "no visit to $i$ in steps $1$ to $n-1$" condition.** This forces $k \neq i$ (otherwise step $n-1$ would itself be a return to $i$). The probability of the trajectory up to $n-1$ ending in $k$ without touching $i$ is exactly $p_{ik}^{(n-1)}$ restricted to such trajectories; by the **Markov property** and the structure of "avoiding $i$", this equals $p_{ik}^{(n-1)}$ in the chain *with $i$ removed* — but for the formal sum, we use the **full** $p_{ik}^{(n-1)}$ because the constraint is built into $f_{ii}^{(n)}$. > [!NOTE] > **Clean formal statement.** For $n \ge 2$, conditioning on the *first step* gives the well-known recurrence $$ f_{ij}^{(n)} \;=\; \sum_{k \neq j} f_{ik}^{(n-1)} \, p_{kj} $$ > with the initial condition $f_{ij}^{(1)} = p_{ij}$. The equation in the proposition above is the specialisation $i = j$. **Step 4 — Conclusion.** Substituting back, we obtain the boxed identity. $$ \boxed{\,f_{ii}^{(n)} \;=\; \sum_{k \neq i} p_{ik}^{(n-1)} \, p_{ki}\,} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Number of Visits to a Transient State **Claim.** If state $j$ is transient, then $$ E[V_j \mid X_0 = i] \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} \;<\; \infty $$ **Step 1 — Decompose $V_j$.** The total number of visits to state $j$ can be written as $$ V_j \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{1}_{\{X_n = j\}} $$ where $\mathbf{1}_{\{\cdot\}}$ is the indicator function. **Step 2 — Take expectations.** By linearity and the definition of $n$-step transition probabilities, $$ E[V_j \mid X_0 = i] \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P(X_n = j \mid X_0 = i) \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ij}^{(n)} $$ **Step 3 — Geometric decay argument.** The chain "attempts" to return to $j$ infinitely many times. Each attempt, after the first, succeeds independently with probability $f_{jj}$. Since $f_{jj} < 1$ (transient), the series of probabilities $p_{jj}^{(n)}$ satisfies the **renewal-type identity** $$ p_{jj}^{(n)} \;=\; f_{jj}^{(n)} \;+\; \sum_{m=1}^{n-1} f_{jj}^{(m)} \, p_{jj}^{(n-m)} $$ (The chain either first-returns at step $m < n$ and then behaves as if restarting from $j$ for the remaining $n - m$ steps.) **Step 4 — Solve the renewal equation.** The **generating function** identity yields $$ \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{jj}^{(n)} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - f_{jj}} $$ Since $f_{jj} < 1$, the right-hand side is a finite positive number — confirming the finiteness of the expected count. **Step 5 — General starting state $i$.** The off-diagonal sum $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ij}^{(n)}$ is bounded by the geometric series $1 / (1 - f_{jj}) < \infty$, completing the proof. $$ \boxed{\,E[V_j \mid X_0 = i] \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - f_{jj}} \;<\; \infty\,} $$ ## 3.3 Worked Example — Complete Analytical Solution > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU-style).** Consider a Markov chain with state space $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and transition matrix $$ P \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ > Classify each state as transient or recurrent, and compute the probability of absorption in state 3 starting from state 1. **Step 1 — Visualise the chain.** $$ \text{State 1} \xleftrightarrow{\;1/2\;} \text{State 2}, \qquad \text{State 1} \xrightarrow{\;1/2\;} \text{State 3 (absorbing)} $$ **Step 2 — Classify State 3.** Since $p_{33} = 1$, once the chain enters state 3, it never leaves. Hence $$ f_{33} \;=\; p_{33}^{(1)} + p_{33}^{(2)} + \dots \;=\; 1 + 1 + \dots \;=\; 1 $$ State 3 is **recurrent** (in fact, absorbing). **Step 3 — Classify State 2.** From state 2, the chain moves to state 1 in one step. From state 1, it either re-enters state 2 (prob $1/2$) or leaves permanently to state 3 (prob $1/2$). Hence the probability of ever returning to state 2 is $$ f_{22} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1 \;+\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \;<\; 1 $$ **State 2 is transient.** **Step 4 — Classify State 1.** From state 1, the chain moves to state 2 or state 3 with probability $1/2$ each. If it moves to state 3, it never returns. If it moves to state 2, it returns to state 1 deterministically in the next step. So $$ f_{11} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1 \;+\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot 0 \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \;<\; 1 $$ **State 1 is also transient.** **Step 5 — Compute absorption probability.** Let $\alpha_i$ denote the probability of eventual absorption in state 3 starting from state $i$. The first-step decomposition gives the system $$ \begin{aligned} \alpha_1 \;&=\; p_{12} \, \alpha_2 \;+\; p_{13} \, \alpha_3 \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\alpha_2 + \frac{1}{2}(1) \\[4pt] \alpha_2 \;&=\; p_{21} \, \alpha_1 \;+\; p_{22} \, \alpha_2 \;+\; p_{23} \, \alpha_3 \;=\; 1 \cdot \alpha_1 + 0 + 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Solve the linear system.** From the second equation, $\alpha_2 = \alpha_1$. Substituting into the first: $$ \alpha_1 \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\alpha_1 + \frac{1}{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2}\alpha_1 = \frac{1}{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; \alpha_1 = 1 $$ Wait — this means the chain *always* reaches state 3 starting from state 1 or 2. Let us verify: from state 1, the chain goes to state 2 or state 3 with equal probability. If it goes to state 3, it is absorbed. If it goes to state 2, it goes back to state 1 (since $p_{21} = 1$). It then repeats. Each visit to state 1 is an independent Bernoulli trial with success probability $1/2$. The probability of *eventual* success is 1. $$ \boxed{\,\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = 1\,} $$ **Step 7 — Verification via the Fundamental Matrix.** Identify $T = \{1, 2\}$ (transient) and $A = \{3\}$ (absorbing). Then $$ Q \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1/2 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad R \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} $$ Compute $I - Q$: $$ I - Q \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1/2 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ The determinant is $(1)(1) - (-1/2)(-1) = 1 - 1/2 = 1/2$. The inverse is $$ N \;=\; (I - Q)^{-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{1/2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1/2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix} $$ The absorption matrix: $$ B \;=\; N R \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1/2 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ This confirms $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = 1$ — every transient starting state is absorbed with probability 1. **Step 8 — Interpret $N$.** The $(1,1)$-entry of $N$ is $2$: starting from state 1, the expected number of visits to state 1 is **2** (the initial visit plus, on average, one return). The $(1,2)$-entry is $1$: the expected number of visits to state 2 is **1** (the chain passes through state 2 at most once on average before absorption). This is a beautiful sanity check. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 State-Transition Diagram with Classification The diagram below is a **Mermaid state-transition diagram** for a three-state chain with one transient class $\{1, 2\}$ feeding into a recurrent absorbing class $\{3\}$. Each node's classification is annotated for KTU exam recall. ```mermaid graph LR S1(("STATE 1
TRANSIENT
f11 = 0.5")) S2(("STATE 2
TRANSIENT
f22 = 0.5")) S3(("STATE 3
RECURRENT
ABSORBING")) S1 -- p=0.5 --> S2 S2 -- p=1.0 --> S1 S1 -- p=0.5 --> S3 S3 -- p=1.0 --> S3 classDef transient fill:#FFD580,stroke:#D97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef recurrent fill:#A7F3D0,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class S1,S2 transient class S3 recurrent ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Orange nodes** are *transient* — the chain may leave them permanently. - **Green node** is *recurrent* (absorbing) — the chain, once entered, stays forever. - Self-loop on State 3 with probability 1 visually confirms the absorbing nature. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Transient-State Analysis The flowchart below maps the **algorithmic procedure** a KTU student should follow to *classify* and *analyse* a transient state. This is the workflow an examiner expects to see in a 14-mark answer. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Markov Chain with P matrix"] --> B["Identify Closed Communicating Classes"] B --> C["Closed Class exists?"] C -- YES --> D["Class is Recurrent (Persistent)"] C -- NO --> E["State is Transient"] D --> F["Finite State Space?"] F -- YES --> G["Positive Recurrent mu less than infinity"] F -- NO --> H["Check mu: finite = Positive, infinite = Null"] E --> I["Partition P into Q and R blocks"] I --> J["Compute N equals I minus Q inverse"] J --> K["N ij equals Expected visits to j from i"] J --> L["B equals N times R equals Absorption Probabilities"] K --> M["Apply to Engineering Problem"] L --> M G --> M H --> M M --> N["END: Solution to KTU Problem"] ``` **Reading the flowchart:** - The **left branch** handles recurrent states (top-down classification: recurrent → positive/null). - The **right branch** handles transient states (downward into the Fundamental Matrix computation). - Both branches converge at **"Apply to Engineering Problem"** — the final interpretive step for full KTU marks. ## 4.3 Decision-Tree for State Classification (Alternative View) ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["State i with P matrix"] --> P1{"Is i in a closed
communicating class?"} P1 -- YES --> P2["Recurrent State
f_ii = 1"] P1 -- NO --> P3["Transient State
f_ii less than 1"] P2 --> P4{"Finite state space?"} P4 -- YES --> P5["Positive Recurrent
mu_i finite"] P4 -- NO --> P6{"Is mu_i finite?"} P6 -- YES --> P7["Positive Recurrent"] P6 -- NO --> P8["Null Recurrent"] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Favourite Shortcut.** In a *finite* state space, a state is **transient** if and only if it is **not** part of any closed communicating class. Identifying closed classes is therefore the **fastest** classification method on the exam. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define a *transient state* of a Markov chain. Give the classification criterion in terms of the return probability $f_{ii}$. **Model Answer:** A state $i$ of a discrete-time Markov chain $\{X_n, n \ge 0\}$ with state space $S$ is called a **transient state** if, starting from $i$, the probability that the chain will *eventually return* to $i$ is strictly less than unity. $$ \boxed{\,i \text{ is transient} \iff f_{ii} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_{ii}^{(n)} < 1\,} $$ Equivalently, the probability of *never* returning to $i$ is $1 - f_{ii} > 0$. A transient state is visited only a *finite* number of times almost surely. In a finite state space, a state is transient if and only if it is **not** contained in any closed communicating class. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q2. (3 Marks)** State and explain the **mean recurrence time** formula. What is its significance in classifying recurrent states? **Model Answer:** The **mean recurrence time** of a state $i$ is defined as the expected number of steps to return to $i$, starting from $i$: $$ \mu_i \;=\; E[T_i \mid X_0 = i] \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n \, f_{ii}^{(n)} $$ where $T_i = \min\{n \ge 1 : X_n = i\}$ is the first-return time. **Significance in classification:** A recurrent state is further classified as: - **Positive recurrent** if $\mu_i < \infty$ (mean return time is finite). - **Null recurrent** if $\mu_i = \infty$ (mean return time is infinite despite $f_{ii} = 1$). For **finite** state-space chains, *every* recurrent state is positive recurrent. Null recurrence can occur **only** in infinite state spaces, a fact frequently tested in KTU exams. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout.** > 1. **Do not confuse transient with "short-lived".** A transient state is *not* one that the chain leaves quickly — it is one the chain may *never* return to. > 2. **Always verify that the absorbing block is correct.** Many students misidentify transient states as recurrent because they forget to check for *closed* communicating classes. > 3. **When computing the fundamental matrix $N = (I - Q)^{-1}$, use exact fractions, not decimal approximations.** A single rounding error propagates and forfeits 2–3 marks. > 4. **The rows of the absorption matrix $B = NR$ must sum to 1** (assuming the chain is started in a transient state). If your row does not sum to 1, recheck your partition. > 5. **Do not forget to state the *classification theorem* explicitly in long-answer questions.** Examiners award 2 marks purely for the *correct statement* of the criterion. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** > **Q3. (a) (7 Marks)** A Markov chain has the state space $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and transition matrix $$ P \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1/3 & 2/3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1/2 & 0 & 0 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} $$ > Classify each state as transient or recurrent, identifying closed communicating classes. Justify your answer with the relevant theorem. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Look for absorbing states.** $p_{33} = 1$ and $p_{44} = 1/2 \neq 1$ — state 3 is absorbing; state 4 is not. **Step 2 — Identify closed classes.** A class $C$ is closed if $\sum_{j \in C} p_{ij} = 1$ for all $i \in C$. Check $\{3\}$: $p_{33} = 1$. **Yes, $\{3\}$ is a closed class (a singleton absorbing state).** Check any other subsets: $\{1, 2\}$: $p_{21} = 0$, so it is not closed. $\{4\}$: $p_{44} = 1/2 < 1$, so not closed. **Step 3 — Apply the classification theorem.** By the Finite-State Classification Theorem, *states belonging to a closed communicating class are recurrent*; *all other states are transient*. - **State 3:** belongs to the closed class $\{3\}$ ⟹ **recurrent (absorbing)**. - **States 1, 2, 4:** do not belong to any closed class ⟹ **transient**. **Step 4 — Verify via return probabilities (optional but recommended).** For state 1: $f_{11} = P(\text{return to 1})$. From 1, the chain can go to 2 (prob $1/3$) or 3 (prob $2/3$). Once in 3, it never returns to 1. From 2, the chain goes to 4 (prob 1). From 4, the chain goes to 1 (prob $1/2$) or stays at 4 (prob $1/2$). This produces a geometric return pattern with $$ f_{11} \;=\; \frac{1}{3} \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1 + (\text{prob of return via multiple 4 visits}) $$ Computing carefully via the renewal equation: $f_{44} = 1/2$, so the expected number of returns to 4 from 4 is $f_{44}/(1-f_{44}) = 1$. The probability of ever returning to 1 from 1 is $f_{11} = (1/3)(1)(1) \cdot 1 = 1/3 < 1$. **State 1 is transient.** ✓ Similarly $f_{22}$ and $f_{44}$ can be shown to be strictly less than 1. **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\text{State 3 — Recurrent (Absorbing); \quad States 1, 2, 4 — Transient.}} $$ > **(b) (7 Marks)** For the chain above, find the **probability of absorption in state 3** starting from each of the transient states 1, 2 and 4 using the fundamental matrix method. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Partition $P$.** Transient states: $T = \{1, 2, 4\}$. Absorbing: $A = \{3\}$. $$ Q \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1/3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1/2 & 0 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad R \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2/3 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} $$ (Reorder transient states as $1, 2, 4$ to match $Q$.) **Step 2 — Compute $I - Q$.** $$ I - Q \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1/3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 \\ -1/2 & 0 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\det(I - Q)$.** Expanding along the first row: $$ \det(I - Q) \;=\; 1 \cdot \det\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} - \left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right) \cdot \det\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ -1/2 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} + 0 $$ $$ = 1 \cdot \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(0 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} - (-1)(-1/2)\right) = \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2} - \tfrac{1}{6} = \tfrac{1}{3} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the cofactor matrix and $N = (I - Q)^{-1}$.** The cofactor $C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ij}$: - $C_{11} = \det\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} = 1/2$ - $C_{12} = -\det\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ -1/2 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} = -(-1/2) = 1/2$ - $C_{13} = \det\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1/2 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = 0 - (-1/2) = 1/2$ - $C_{21} = -\det\begin{pmatrix} -1/3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} = -(-1/6) = 1/6$ - $C_{22} = \det\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ -1/2 & 1/2 \end{pmatrix} = 1/2$ - $C_{23} = -\det\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1/3 \\ -1/2 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = -(0 - 1/6) = 1/6$ - $C_{31} = \det\begin{pmatrix} -1/3 & 0 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = 1/3 - 0 = 1/3$ - $C_{32} = -\det\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = -(-1) = 1$ - $C_{33} = \det\begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1/3 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = 1$ The cofactor matrix is $$ C \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 1/6 & 1/2 & 1/6 \\ 1/3 & 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ The adjugate is the transpose: $\text{adj}(I - Q) = C^{T}$. Then $$ N \;=\; (I - Q)^{-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{\det(I - Q)} \cdot C^{T} \;=\; 3 \cdot C^{T} $$ $$ N \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 3/2 & 1/2 & 1 \\ 3/2 & 3/2 & 3 \\ 3/2 & 1/2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Compute $B = N R$.** $$ B \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 3/2 & 1/2 & 1 \\ 3/2 & 3/2 & 3 \\ 3/2 & 1/2 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2/3 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} (3/2)(2/3) \\ (3/2)(2/3) \\ (3/2)(2/3) \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 6 — Interpret.** Starting from **any** transient state (1, 2, or 4), the chain is absorbed in state 3 with probability 1. (The chain cannot escape the transient block without eventually reaching the absorbing state.) $$ \boxed{\,P(\text{absorbed in 3} \mid X_0 = 1) = P(\text{absorbed in 3} \mid X_0 = 2) = P(\text{absorbed in 3} \mid X_0 = 4) = 1\,} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]** > **Q4. (a) (7 Marks)** Consider a Markov chain on $S = \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ with transition matrix $$ P \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1/4 & 1/4 & 1/4 & 1/4 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 0 & 0 & 2/3 & 1/3 \end{pmatrix} $$ > Identify the transient and recurrent states. Compute the **expected number of visits** to each transient state starting from transient state 1. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Look for absorbing states.** $p_{00} = 1$. **State 0 is absorbing ⟹ recurrent.** **Step 2 — Check the class $\{2, 3\}$.** Compute $P(2 \to 2) = 1/2$, $P(2 \to 3) = 1/2$, $P(3 \to 2) = 2/3$, $P(3 \to 3) = 1/3$. The sub-matrix on $\{2, 3\}$ is $$ P_{\{2,3\}} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 1/2 \\ 2/3 & 1/3 \end{pmatrix} $$ Both rows sum to 1, and the matrix is stochastic. **The class $\{2, 3\}$ is closed (no transitions out of $\{2, 3\}$) and irreducible (both states communicate).** Hence **both states 2 and 3 are recurrent**. **Step 3 — Classify state 1.** State 1 transitions to $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ with positive probabilities. Once it leaves to 0 (absorbing), 2, or 3 (recurrent class), it cannot return. **State 1 is transient.** **Classification summary:** State 0 (recurrent absorbing), States 2, 3 (recurrent non-absorbing — they form a closed irreducible class), State 1 (transient). **Step 4 — Set up the fundamental matrix.** Transient states: $T = \{1\}$. (Only state 1 is transient.) So $Q$ is a $1 \times 1$ matrix: $$ Q \;=\; (p_{11}) \;=\; (1/4) $$ **Step 5 — Compute $N = (1 - Q)^{-1}$.** $$ N \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - 1/4} \;=\; \frac{1}{3/4} \;=\; \frac{4}{3} $$ **Step 6 — Interpret.** The expected number of visits to transient state 1 starting from transient state 1 is $$ E[V_1 \mid X_0 = 1] \;=\; N_{11} \;=\; \frac{4}{3} $$ This means, on average, the chain visits state 1 about **1.33 times** before leaving permanently. The first visit is the starting visit; on average, the chain returns to 1 approximately $1/3$ of a time before being absorbed or escaping to the recurrent class. $$ \boxed{\,E[V_1 \mid X_0 = 1] = \dfrac{4}{3}\,} $$ > **(b) (7 Marks)** Compute the **probability of absorption in state 0** starting from state 1, and the **probability of entering the recurrent class $\{2, 3\}$** starting from state 1. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the absorption sub-matrix $R$.** From the single transient state 1, the chain moves to 0 with probability $1/4$, to 2 with probability $1/4$, and to 3 with probability $1/4$. (It also stays at 1 with probability $1/4$, captured by $Q$.) So $$ R \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1/4 & 1/4 & 1/4 \end{pmatrix} $$ where the columns correspond to states 0, 2, 3 respectively. **Step 2 — Compute $B = N R$.** $$ B \;=\; \frac{4}{3} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 1/4 & 1/4 & 1/4 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 1/3 & 1/3 & 1/3 \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 3 — Interpret the entries.** - **Absorption in state 0:** $\;P(\text{absorbed in 0} \mid X_0 = 1) = 1/3.$ - **Entering recurrent class $\{2, 3\}$:** The probability of eventually reaching state 2 is $1/3$ and state 3 is $1/3$. The total probability of *entering* the class $\{2, 3\}$ is $$ P(\text{ever reach } \{2, 3\} \mid X_0 = 1) \;=\; 1/3 + 1/3 \;=\; 2/3 $$ **Step 4 — Sanity check.** The three probabilities must sum to 1 (since the chain must eventually leave state 1 and either be absorbed at 0 or enter $\{2, 3\}$): $$ 1/3 + 2/3 \;=\; 1 \;\;\checkmark $$ $$ \boxed{\,P(\text{absorbed in 0} \mid X_0 = 1) = \frac{1}{3}, \quad P(\text{enter } \{2,3\} \mid X_0 = 1) = \frac{2}{3}\,} $$ **Step 5 — Verification via first-step analysis.** Let $h_i = P(\text{ever reach 0} \mid X_0 = i)$. The first-step equations are $$ h_0 = 1, \quad h_1 = (1/4) h_0 + (1/4) h_1 + (1/4) h_2 + (1/4) h_3 $$ Since states 2 and 3 are recurrent but **not** absorbing in 0, $h_2 = h_3 = 0$ (once in $\{2, 3\}$, the chain never leaves). Therefore $$ h_1 \;=\; (1/4)(1) + (1/4)h_1 + 0 + 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (3/4) h_1 = 1/4 \;\Longrightarrow\; h_1 = 1/3 $$ This **confirms** the result obtained via the fundamental matrix. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transient States** - **Definition (Memorise).** State $i$ is *transient* iff $f_{ii} = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_{ii}^{(n)} < 1$. Equivalently, the chain has a *positive* probability of *never* returning to $i$. - **Companion Definition.** State $i$ is *recurrent* iff $f_{ii} = 1$. - **Finite-State Theorem.** In a *finite* state-space chain, a state is transient **iff** it is **not** in any closed communicating class. - **Mean Recurrence Time.** $\mu_i = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n f_{ii}^{(n)}$. Positive recurrent ⟺ $\mu_i < \infty$. - **Null Recurrence.** Exists **only** in infinite state spaces; never in finite chains. - **Expected Visits.** $E[V_j \mid X_0 = i] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_{ij}^{(n)}$; for transient $j$, this equals $\frac{1}{1 - f_{jj}} < \infty$. - **Fundamental Matrix.** $N = (I - Q)^{-1}$; $(N)_{ij}$ = expected number of visits to $j$ from $i$. - **Absorption Matrix.** $B = NR$; $(B)_{ij}$ = probability that the chain, starting in transient $i$, eventually reaches recurrent $j$. - **First-Return Recurrence.** $f_{ii}^{(n)} = \sum_{k \neq i} p_{ik}^{(n-1)} p_{ki}$ for $n \ge 2$; with $f_{ii}^{(1)} = p_{ii}$. - **Geometric Insight.** Once a transient state is left, the *probability* of returning eventually is $f_{ii} < 1$, so the *number* of returns is a geometric random variable with mean $f_{ii} / (1 - f_{ii})$. - **Exam Strategy.** Always *first* identify closed classes (faster than computing $f_{ii}$), *then* verify with the return-probability formula for full marks. - **Verification Trick.** Rows of $B$ should sum to 1; if not, recheck your partition of $P$ into $Q$ and $R$. - **Real-World Lens.** PageRank, software reliability, MCMC burn-in, TCP retransmissions, queue warm-up — all rely on transient-state analysis.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GAMAT301 - MATHEMATICS FOR COMPUTER AND INFORMATION SCIENCE-3
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Long-Run Proportions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long-Run Proportion (KTU Definition)**
> For a finite, irreducible, and aperiodic Markov chain with state space $S = \{0, 1, 2, \dots, m\}$ and transition probability matrix $\mathbf{P} = [P_{ij}]$, the **long-run proportion** of time the chain spends in state $j$ is the limiting probability:
> $$\pi_j = \lim_{n \to \infty} P_{ij}^{(n)}$$
> The vector $\boldsymbol{\pi} = (\pi_0, \pi_1, \dots, \pi_m)$ is called the **stationary distribution** and is the unique non-negative solution of the system:
> $$\boldsymbol{\pi} \, \mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}, \quad \sum_{j=0}^{m} \pi_j = 1$$

In plain words: as we let the chain run for a very large number of steps $n$, the chance of finding it in state $j$ stops depending on time and on the starting state $i$. That stabilized probability is the **long-run proportion** of state $j$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Classroom Footfall" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy**
> Imagine a small **2-room office** (Room A and Room B). Every morning, a worker follows a rule: *"If I am in Room A today, there is a 70% chance I will be in A tomorrow, 30% chance in B; if I am in B, there is 40% chance in A tomorrow, 60% in B."* If you observe the worker for **10 days**, the percentage of days spent in Room A depends heavily on where he started. But if you observe him for **10,000 days**, the percentage converges to a fixed number — say **$\frac{4}{7} \approx 57.14\%$**. This stable, start-independent number is the **long-run proportion** $\pi_A = 4/7$. It is NOT a prediction of *when* he is in A, but rather *what fraction of the long-term time* he spends in A.

## 1.3 Why "Long-Run" and Not "Short-Run"?

- **Short-run** ($n$ small): the chain's distribution depends on the starting state $i$. Different starts give different $P_{ij}^{(n)}$.
- **Long-run** ($n \to \infty$): the **memory of the starting state is lost** (a property called *mixing*). The proportions stabilize to a constant $\pi_j$ — independent of $i$, and independent of $n$.

> [!TIP]
> **Three Prerequisite Conditions for Long-Run Proportions to Exist**
> 1. **Finite state space** — the chain must have a finite (or countable) number of states.
> 2. **Irreducible** — every state can be reached from every other state (the chain forms one communicating class).
> 3. **Aperiodic** — the chain must not get trapped in cycles of fixed parity (e.g., even-numbered-step returns).
>
> If even one condition fails, the limit $\lim_{n\to\infty} P_{ij}^{(n)}$ may not exist, or may depend on $i$.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics Highlighted in This Module

- **Convergence rate:** how fast $P_{ij}^{(n)}$ approaches $\pi_j$ — governed by the **second-largest eigenvalue** of $\mathbf{P}$.
- **Mean recurrence time:** $m_j = 1/\pi_j$ — the average number of steps between successive visits to state $j$ in the long run.
- **Ergodic (steady-state) probability:** another name for the long-run proportion $\pi_j$, used in queueing, reliability, and page-rank theory.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Theorem (Fundamental Limit Theorem for Markov Chains)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Existence of Long-Run Proportions)**
> Let $\{X_n\}$ be a Markov chain with finite state space $S$ and transition matrix $\mathbf{P}$. If the chain is **irreducible** and **aperiodic**, then:
> 1. There exists a unique probability vector $\boldsymbol{\pi} = (\pi_j)_{j \in S}$ such that $\pi_j > 0$ for every state $j$ and $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$.
> 2. For every pair of states $i, j \in S$:
> $$\lim_{n \to \infty} P_{ij}^{(n)} = \pi_j$$
> 3. The long-run proportion of time the chain spends in state $j$ (with probability 1) equals $\pi_j$, regardless of the initial state.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Structure

**Step 1 — Form the balance equations.**
For each state $j$, the inflow of probability must equal the outflow in steady state:
$$\sum_{i \in S} \pi_i \, P_{ij} = \pi_j \quad \forall \, j \in S$$
This is exactly the row-equation $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$.

**Step 2 — Impose the probability constraint.**
The components of $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ must sum to **1** (it is a probability distribution):
$$\sum_{j \in S} \pi_j = 1$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system.**
The balance equations yield a **homogeneous system** $(\mathbf{P}^\top - \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \mathbf{0}$. The normalization $\sum \pi_j = 1$ produces the unique solution because the matrix $\mathbf{P}^\top - \mathbf{I}$ is singular with a one-dimensional null space (under irreducibility).

**Step 4 — Verify positivity.**
Check that every $\pi_j > 0$. If some $\pi_j = 0$, the chain is **reducible** — the long-run proportion for that state is zero because the chain permanently leaves that class.

**Step 5 — Interpret the solution.**
$\pi_j$ is the **long-run fraction of time** the chain visits state $j$. Equivalently, $1/\pi_j$ is the **expected recurrence time** of state $j$ (mean return period).

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why" Layer)

| Step | Purpose | What Goes Wrong If Skipped |
|---|---|---|
| Balance equations | Encode conservation of probability in steady state | No mechanism to find $\pi$ |
| Normalization | Pins the scale of the homogeneous solution | Only the *ratio* of $\pi_j$ is determined |
| Solve system | Produces unique numerical vector | Cannot proceed to engineering interpretation |
| Positivity check | Confirms irreducibility assumption | If some $\pi_j = 0$, theorem does not apply |
| Interpretation | Connects math to physical fraction of time | A correct number with no meaning = no marks |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Long-Run Proportions (KTU Board Exam Essentials)**

| # | Formula / Statement | Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ | Stationary (balance) equation | All Markov chains |
| 2 | $\sum_{j=0}^{m} \pi_j = 1$ | Normalization of probability vector | Always required |
| 3 | $\pi_j = \lim_{n\to\infty} P_{ij}^{(n)}$ | Definition of long-run proportion | Irreducible + aperiodic |
| 4 | $m_j = 1/\pi_j$ | Mean recurrence time of state $j$ | Irreducible + aperiodic, $\pi_j>0$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{P}^n \to \mathbf{1}\boldsymbol{\pi}$ as $n \to \infty$ | Matrix form of convergence | Irreducible + aperiodic |
| 6 | $\pi_j > 0 \, \forall j \in S$ | Positivity criterion | Equivalent to irreducibility |
| 7 | Period $d(j) = \gcd\{n \geq 1 : P_{jj}^{(n)}>0\}$ | Tests aperiodicity | $d(j)=1 \Rightarrow$ aperiodic |

> **Critical LaTeX safety note:** in handwritten answers, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ for absolute value — never use the bare vertical bar $\vert$ which conflicts with markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Queueing Theory (M/M/1 queues):** $\pi_n$ gives the long-run fraction of time the system has $n$ customers — used in call-centre staffing and router-buffer design.
- **PageRank Algorithm (Google Search):** the long-run proportion is the random-surfer stationary distribution over the web-graph.
- **Reliability Engineering:** long-run proportion of time a system is *up* versus *down* defines its **availability** $\pi_{\text{up}}$.
- **Weather Forecasting:** chains with states {Sunny, Cloudy, Rainy} — $\pi_{\text{rain}}$ is the climatological fraction of rainy days.
- **Genetic Drift Models:** Wright–Fisher chain — long-run proportions describe allele fixation probabilities.
- **Network Protocol Analysis:** long-run proportion of channel *idle* slots drives throughput formulas in ALOHA / CSMA.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example A — Two-State Chain (Full Derivation)

**Problem statement.** A worker alternates between two office rooms $A$ and $B$ daily. Transition rules:
- If in $A$ today, stays in $A$ with probability **$0.7$**, moves to $B$ with probability **$0.3$**.
- If in $B$ today, moves to $A$ with probability **$0.4$**, stays in $B$ with probability **$0.6$**.

Find the long-run proportion of days spent in each room.

### Step 1 — Write the transition matrix
The transition matrix $\mathbf{P}$ with states ordered as $(A, B)$ is:
$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{pmatrix} P_{AA} & P_{AB} \\ P_{BA} & P_{BB} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Verify irreducibility and aperiodicity
- **Irreducible:** $P_{AB}=0.3>0$ and $P_{BA}=0.4>0$, so $A \to B$ and $B \to A$ are both possible. Single communicating class. ✓
- **Aperiodic:** $P_{AA}=0.7>0$ gives return-to-$A$ in 1 step, so period $d(A)=1$. By irreducibility, all states are aperiodic. ✓

Hence the long-run proportion $\pi_j$ exists and is unique.

### Step 3 — Write the balance equations
Let $\pi_A$ and $\pi_B$ be the long-run proportions. The row-equation $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P}=\boldsymbol{\pi}$ gives:
$$\pi_A \cdot 0.7 + \pi_B \cdot 0.4 = \pi_A$$
$$\pi_A \cdot 0.3 + \pi_B \cdot 0.6 = \pi_B$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the first equation
Move $\pi_A$ from the right to the left:
$$\pi_A \cdot 0.7 + \pi_B \cdot 0.4 - \pi_A = 0$$
$$\pi_A (0.7 - 1) + \pi_B \cdot 0.4 = 0$$
$$-0.3 \, \pi_A + 0.4 \, \pi_B = 0$$
$$0.3 \, \pi_A = 0.4 \, \pi_B$$
$$\pi_A = \frac{0.4}{0.3} \, \pi_B = \frac{4}{3} \, \pi_B$$

### Step 5 — Apply normalization
The probabilities must sum to 1:
$$\pi_A + \pi_B = 1$$
Substitute $\pi_A = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi_B$:
$$\frac{4}{3} \pi_B + \pi_B = 1$$
$$\frac{4}{3}\pi_B + \frac{3}{3}\pi_B = 1$$
$$\frac{7}{3} \pi_B = 1$$
$$\pi_B = \frac{3}{7}$$
$$\pi_A = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{7} = \frac{4}{7}$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation
$$\boxed{\pi_A = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.5714, \qquad \pi_B = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286}$$
The worker spends roughly **57.14%** of all days in room $A$ and **42.86%** of all days in room $B$ — irrespective of which room he started in.

### Step 7 — Mean recurrence times
Using the formula $m_j = 1/\pi_j$:
$$m_A = \frac{1}{4/7} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75 \text{ days}, \quad m_B = \frac{1}{3/7} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.33 \text{ days}$$
On average, the worker returns to $A$ every **1.75 days** and to $B$ every **2.33 days**. Sanity check: $1.75 + 2.33 = 4.08 \approx 2 \cdot 2.04$ ✓ (cycle length is consistent).

### Step 8 — Verify using $\mathbf{P}^n$ (optional, for higher marks)
Compute $\mathbf{P}^2$:
$$\mathbf{P}^2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.7 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.61 & 0.39 \\ 0.52 & 0.48 \end{pmatrix}$$
Note: each column is approaching $(4/7, 3/7) \approx (0.5714, 0.4286)$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — Three-State Weather Chain

**Problem statement.** A city's daily weather follows a 3-state Markov chain with states $\{S=\text{Sunny},\, C=\text{Cloudy},\, R=\text{Rainy}\}$ and transition matrix:
$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.3 & 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$
Find the long-run proportion of sunny, cloudy and rainy days.

### Step 1 — Verify conditions
All entries of $\mathbf{P}$ are strictly positive, so the chain is automatically **irreducible and aperiodic**. Long-run proportion exists and is unique.

### Step 2 — Write the balance equations
Let $\pi_S, \pi_C, \pi_R$ denote the long-run proportions. From $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P}=\boldsymbol{\pi}$:
$$0.6 \pi_S + 0.3 \pi_C + 0.2 \pi_R = \pi_S$$
$$0.3 \pi_S + 0.4 \pi_C + 0.3 \pi_R = \pi_C$$
$$0.1 \pi_S + 0.3 \pi_C + 0.5 \pi_R = \pi_R$$
plus $\pi_S + \pi_C + \pi_R = 1$.

### Step 3 — Simplify
Rearrange each balance equation by moving the left side to the right:
$$-0.4 \pi_S + 0.3 \pi_C + 0.2 \pi_R = 0$$
$$0.3 \pi_S - 0.6 \pi_C + 0.3 \pi_R = 0$$
$$0.1 \pi_S + 0.3 \pi_C - 0.5 \pi_R = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve the homogeneous system
Adding all three equations:
$$0 \cdot \pi_S + 0 \cdot \pi_C + 0 \cdot \pi_R = 0$$
(consistent with linear dependence expected for balance equations — only the *ratios* are determined).

From equation 2:
$$0.3 \pi_S + 0.3 \pi_R = 0.6 \pi_C$$
$$\pi_S + \pi_R = 2 \pi_C$$

From equation 1:
$$0.4 \pi_S = 0.3 \pi_C + 0.2 \pi_R$$
$$\pi_S = 0.75 \pi_C + 0.5 \pi_R$$

From equation 3:
$$0.5 \pi_R = 0.1 \pi_S + 0.3 \pi_C$$
$$\pi_R = 0.2 \pi_S + 0.6 \pi_C$$

Substitute $\pi_S = 0.75\pi_C + 0.5\pi_R$ into the third:
$$\pi_R = 0.2(0.75\pi_C + 0.5\pi_R) + 0.6\pi_C$$
$$\pi_R = 0.15\pi_C + 0.1\pi_R + 0.6\pi_C$$
$$\pi_R - 0.1 \pi_R = 0.75 \pi_C$$
$$0.9 \pi_R = 0.75 \pi_C$$
$$\pi_R = \frac{0.75}{0.9} \pi_C = \frac{5}{6} \pi_C$$

Then:
$$\pi_S = 0.75\pi_C + 0.5 \cdot \frac{5}{6}\pi_C = 0.75\pi_C + \frac{5}{12}\pi_C = \frac{9}{12}\pi_C + \frac{5}{12}\pi_C = \frac{14}{12}\pi_C = \frac{7}{6}\pi_C$$

### Step 5 — Apply normalization
$$\pi_S + \pi_C + \pi_R = 1$$
$$\frac{7}{6}\pi_C + \pi_C + \frac{5}{6}\pi_C = 1$$
$$\frac{7}{6}\pi_C + \frac{6}{6}\pi_C + \frac{5}{6}\pi_C = 1$$
$$\frac{18}{6}\pi_C = 1$$
$$3 \pi_C = 1$$
$$\pi_C = \frac{1}{3}$$

Therefore:
$$\pi_S = \frac{7}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{7}{18}, \quad \pi_C = \frac{1}{3} = \frac{6}{18}, \quad \pi_R = \frac{5}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{5}{18}$$

### Step 6 — Final result
$$\boxed{\pi_S = \frac{7}{18} \approx 0.3889, \quad \pi_C = \frac{6}{18} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333, \quad \pi_R = \frac{5}{18} \approx 0.2778}$$
Check: $\tfrac{7}{18}+\tfrac{6}{18}+\tfrac{5}{18}=\tfrac{18}{18}=1$ ✓

**Mean recurrence times:**
$$m_S = \frac{18}{7} \approx 2.57 \text{ days}, \quad m_C = 3 \text{ days}, \quad m_R = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6 \text{ days}$$
A sunny day returns on average every **2.57 days**, a rainy day every **3.6 days**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Insight Summary (Why This Calculation Works)

The long-run proportion is essentially a **left eigenvector** of $\mathbf{P}$ with eigenvalue 1. The Perron–Frobenius theorem guarantees that for an irreducible, aperiodic, finite stochastic matrix:
- Eigenvalue 1 is **simple** (algebraic multiplicity 1).
- The corresponding left eigenvector can be normalized to a probability vector.
- All other eigenvalues have modulus **strictly less than 1**, so $\mathbf{P}^n$ converges to the rank-one matrix $\mathbf{1}\boldsymbol{\pi}$ as $n \to \infty$.

This is the deep reason why the balance equations $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P}=\boldsymbol{\pi}$ have a **unique** non-negative normalized solution.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "long-run proportion" of a state in a finite Markov chain. State the conditions under which it exists.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** For a finite Markov chain with transition matrix $\mathbf{P} = [P_{ij}]$, the long-run proportion of state $j$ is defined as the limit
> $$\pi_j = \lim_{n \to \infty} P_{ij}^{(n)}$$
> where $P_{ij}^{(n)}$ is the $(i,j)$ entry of $\mathbf{P}^n$. It represents the long-term fraction of time the chain spends in state $j$, irrespective of the starting state $i$.

> **Conditions for existence (1.5 marks):**
> 1. The state space must be **finite**.
> 2. The chain must be **irreducible** (one communicating class).
> 3. The chain must be **aperiodic** (period $d(j) = 1$ for all $j$).

When all three hold, $\pi_j$ is unique, strictly positive, and independent of the starting state $i$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the relation between long-run proportions and mean recurrence time. Compute the mean recurrence time for the worker in Example A above.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Relation (2 marks):** The long-run proportion $\pi_j$ and the **mean recurrence time** $m_j$ of state $j$ are reciprocally related:
> $$m_j = \frac{1}{\pi_j}, \quad \pi_j > 0$$
> Intuitively, if the chain spends a larger fraction of time in $j$, the average gap between successive visits to $j$ must be smaller.

> **Computation (1 mark):** From Example A, $\pi_A = 4/7$, so $m_A = 7/4 = 1.75$ days. $\pi_B = 3/7$, so $m_B = 7/3 \approx 2.33$ days.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization $\sum \pi_j = 1$.** Many students solve the balance equations and stop, giving only the *ratios*. The chain of balance equations is homogeneous — without $\sum \pi_j = 1$, the answer is *incomplete* and you lose up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the irreducibility/aperiodicity check.** KTU examiners specifically allocate 1–2 marks for verifying these conditions. Writing the theorem and skipping the verification is a common error pattern.
> 3. **Sign errors in matrix manipulation.** When you transpose $\mathbf{P}$ to solve $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$, ensure you read the *rows* of $\mathbf{P}$ correctly. Use the row-equation form to avoid confusion.
> 4. **Not interpreting the answer.** A correct number without a sentence saying "the chain spends 57.14% of the time in state A" loses the application mark.
> 5. **Mixing up long-run proportion with steady-state of a differential equation.** This is a *discrete-time* Markov chain problem — there are no derivatives, no time-constants, no $e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024, Module 4]**

A Markov chain has state space $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ with transition matrix:
$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 & 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.4 & 0.2 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.4 & 0.3 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(a)** Show that the chain is irreducible and aperiodic. Hence prove that the long-run proportion $\pi_j$ exists for every state $j$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the long-run proportions $\pi_1, \pi_2, \pi_3$ and the mean recurrence time of state 2. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Irreducibility (3 marks).**
By inspection, every entry of $\mathbf{P}$ is strictly positive:
$$P_{ij} > 0 \quad \text{for all } i, j \in \{1, 2, 3\}$$
Hence from any state $i$, every other state $j$ can be reached in **one step**. The chain has a single communicating class, so it is **irreducible**. ✓

**Step 2 — Aperiodicity (2 marks).**
For state 1: $P_{11} = 0.2 > 0$, so state 1 can return to itself in $n = 1$ step. Thus
$$d(1) = \gcd\{n \geq 1 : P_{11}^{(n)} > 0\}$$
Since 1 is in the set, $\gcd$ must divide 1, giving $d(1) = 1$. By irreducibility, $d(j) = 1$ for every $j \in S$. Hence the chain is **aperiodic**. ✓

**Step 3 — Existence of long-run proportions (2 marks).**
By the Fundamental Limit Theorem for finite Markov chains, a chain that is irreducible and aperiodic admits a unique strictly positive probability vector $\boldsymbol{\pi} = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \pi_3)$ with
$$\pi_j = \lim_{n \to \infty} P_{ij}^{(n)} > 0 \quad \forall \, i, j \in S$$
independent of the initial state. Hence $\pi_j$ exists and is unique for $j = 1, 2, 3$. ∎

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Set up the balance equations (2 marks).**
From $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$:
$$0.2 \pi_1 + 0.4 \pi_2 + 0.3 \pi_3 = \pi_1$$
$$0.5 \pi_1 + 0.2 \pi_2 + 0.4 \pi_3 = \pi_2$$
$$0.3 \pi_1 + 0.4 \pi_2 + 0.3 \pi_3 = \pi_3$$

**Step 2 — Simplify (1 mark).**
$$-0.8 \pi_1 + 0.4 \pi_2 + 0.3 \pi_3 = 0 \quad \text{(Eq. I)}$$
$$0.5 \pi_1 - 0.8 \pi_2 + 0.4 \pi_3 = 0 \quad \text{(Eq. II)}$$
$$0.3 \pi_1 + 0.4 \pi_2 - 0.7 \pi_3 = 0 \quad \text{(Eq. III)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system (3 marks).**
Adding all three equations: $0 = 0$ (linearly dependent, as expected).

From Eq. I: $0.8 \pi_1 = 0.4 \pi_2 + 0.3 \pi_3 \Rightarrow \pi_1 = 0.5 \pi_2 + 0.375 \pi_3$.

From Eq. III: $0.7 \pi_3 = 0.3 \pi_1 + 0.4 \pi_2$. Substitute $\pi_1$:
$$0.7 \pi_3 = 0.3 (0.5 \pi_2 + 0.375 \pi_3) + 0.4 \pi_2$$
$$0.7 \pi_3 = 0.15 \pi_2 + 0.1125 \pi_3 + 0.4 \pi_2$$
$$0.7 \pi_3 - 0.1125 \pi_3 = 0.55 \pi_2$$
$$0.5875 \pi_3 = 0.55 \pi_2$$
$$\pi_2 = \frac{0.5875}{0.55} \pi_3 = \frac{47}{44} \pi_3$$

Then $\pi_1 = 0.5 \cdot \frac{47}{44} \pi_3 + 0.375 \pi_3 = \frac{47}{88} \pi_3 + \frac{33}{88} \pi_3 = \frac{80}{88} \pi_3 = \frac{10}{11} \pi_3$.

**Step 4 — Apply normalization (1 mark).**
$$\pi_1 + \pi_2 + \pi_3 = 1$$
$$\frac{10}{11}\pi_3 + \frac{47}{44}\pi_3 + \pi_3 = 1$$
$$\frac{40}{44}\pi_3 + \frac{47}{44}\pi_3 + \frac{44}{44}\pi_3 = 1$$
$$\frac{131}{44}\pi_3 = 1$$
$$\pi_3 = \frac{44}{131}$$

Therefore:
$$\pi_1 = \frac{10}{11} \cdot \frac{44}{131} = \frac{40}{131}, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{47}{44} \cdot \frac{44}{131} = \frac{47}{131}, \quad \pi_3 = \frac{44}{131}$$

**Final answer (in boxed form, marks for interpretation):**
$$\boxed{\pi_1 = \frac{40}{131} \approx 0.3053, \quad \pi_2 = \frac{47}{131} \approx 0.3588, \quad \pi_3 = \frac{44}{131} \approx 0.3359}$$
**Interpretation:** The chain spends about **30.5%** of time in state 1, **35.9%** in state 2, and **33.6%** in state 3 — irrespective of the starting state.

**Mean recurrence time of state 2:**
$$m_2 = \frac{1}{\pi_2} = \frac{131}{47} \approx 2.787 \text{ steps}$$

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024, Module 4 — Alternative]**

Consider a 2-state Markov chain representing a machine's daily status: $U$ = **Up (working)**, $D$ = **Down (failed)**. The transition matrix is
$$\mathbf{P} = \begin{pmatrix} P_{UU} & P_{UD} \\ P_{DU} & P_{DD} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.85 & 0.15 \\ 0.40 & 0.60 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(a)** Verify the conditions for the existence of long-run proportions. Compute $\pi_U$ and $\pi_D$ symbolically in terms of $P_{UD}$ and $P_{DU}$, then evaluate them numerically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The **long-run availability** of the machine is defined as $A = \pi_U$. Suppose the engineer wants $A \geq 0.95$. Find the minimum value of $P_{UU}$ (the one-day reliability) required to meet this target, assuming $P_{DU} = 0.40$ is fixed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Verify conditions (2 marks).**
All entries are positive: $P_{UU}=0.85>0$, $P_{UD}=0.15>0$, $P_{DU}=0.40>0$, $P_{DD}=0.60>0$.
- $U \to D$ possible ($P_{UD}=0.15$) and $D \to U$ possible ($P_{DU}=0.40$).
- $P_{UU} = 0.85 > 0 \Rightarrow d(U) = 1$, hence aperiodic. By irreducibility, $d(D) = 1$ as well. ✓
- Finite state space $\{U, D\}$. ✓
All three conditions hold — long-run proportion exists.

**Step 2 — Symbolic derivation (3 marks).**
Balance equation for state $U$:
$$P_{UU} \pi_U + P_{DU} \pi_D = \pi_U$$
$$P_{DU} \pi_D = (1 - P_{UU}) \pi_U = P_{UD} \pi_U$$
(using $P_{UD} = 1 - P_{UU}$ and $P_{DU} = 1 - P_{DD}$)
$$\pi_D = \frac{P_{UD}}{P_{DU}} \pi_U$$

Normalize:
$$\pi_U + \pi_D = 1 \Rightarrow \pi_U \left(1 + \frac{P_{UD}}{P_{DU}}\right) = 1$$
$$\pi_U \cdot \frac{P_{DU} + P_{UD}}{P_{DU}} = 1$$
$$\boxed{\pi_U = \frac{P_{DU}}{P_{DU} + P_{UD}}, \quad \pi_D = \frac{P_{UD}}{P_{DU} + P_{UD}}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks).**
With $P_{UD} = 0.15$, $P_{DU} = 0.40$:
$$\pi_U = \frac{0.40}{0.40 + 0.15} = \frac{0.40}{0.55} = \frac{8}{11} \approx 0.7273$$
$$\pi_D = \frac{0.15}{0.55} = \frac{3}{11} \approx 0.2727$$
Sanity check: $\frac{8}{11} + \frac{3}{11} = 1$ ✓

**Interpretation:** The machine is up **72.73%** of the time and down **27.27%** of the time in the long run.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Set up the inequality (2 marks).**
We require:
$$A = \pi_U \geq 0.95$$
Using the formula derived above with $P_{DU} = 0.40$ fixed and $P_{UD} = 1 - P_{UU}$ variable:
$$\frac{P_{DU}}{P_{DU} + P_{UD}} \geq 0.95$$
$$\frac{0.40}{0.40 + (1 - P_{UU})} \geq 0.95$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $P_{UU}$ (3 marks).**
Let $x = P_{UU}$ for brevity. Then $1 - x = P_{UD}$:
$$\frac{0.40}{0.40 + 1 - x} \geq 0.95$$
$$\frac{0.40}{1.40 - x} \geq 0.95$$

Since $0.40 > 0$ and $1.40 - x > 0$ (for feasibility), we can cross-multiply preserving the inequality:
$$0.40 \geq 0.95(1.40 - x)$$
$$0.40 \geq 1.33 - 0.95 x$$
$$0.95 x \geq 1.33 - 0.40$$
$$0.95 x \geq 0.93$$
$$x \geq \frac{0.93}{0.95} = \frac{93}{95} \approx 0.9789$$

**Step 3 — State the conclusion (2 marks).**
$$\boxed{P_{UU} \geq \frac{93}{95} \approx 0.9789}$$
The engineer must ensure the one-day reliability is at least **97.89%** to achieve a long-run availability of 95%, given that the daily repair probability is fixed at 40%.

**Verification (optional):**
At $P_{UU} = 0.9789$, $P_{UD} = 0.0211$. Then
$$\pi_U = \frac{0.40}{0.40 + 0.0211} = \frac{0.40}{0.4211} \approx 0.9499 \approx 0.95 \ \checkmark$$
(slight discrepancy from rounding).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Long-Run Proportions (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** $\pi_j = \lim_{n \to \infty} P_{ij}^{(n)}$ is the long-run fraction of time spent in state $j$ — **independent** of starting state $i$ once the conditions below are met.
- **Existence requires three conditions:** **finite** state space, **irreducibility** (one communicating class), and **aperiodicity** (period = 1).
- **Core equation:** $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ (balance / stationary equation) — the *row* vector $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ is a **left eigenvector** of $\mathbf{P}$ with eigenvalue 1.
- **Normalization:** always append $\sum_{j} \pi_j = 1$ — this is what selects the unique solution from the one-parameter family of eigenvectors.
- **Uniqueness + positivity:** Perron–Frobenius theorem guarantees a unique strictly positive $\pi_j > 0$ for every state.
- **Mean recurrence time:** $m_j = 1/\pi_j$ — average number of steps between successive visits to $j$.
- **Matrix form of convergence:** $\mathbf{P}^n \to \mathbf{1}\boldsymbol{\pi}$ as $n \to \infty$, where $\mathbf{1}$ is the column vector of all ones.
- **2-state shortcut formula:** $\pi_U = \dfrac{P_{DU}}{P_{DU} + P_{UD}}, \quad \pi_D = \dfrac{P_{UD}}{P_{DU} + P_{UD}}$ — memorize and verify with normalization.
- **Common KTU exam traps:** forgetting the normalization, skipping the irreducibility/aperiodicity check, not interpreting the answer in words, mixing up $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P}$ vs. $\mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\pi}$ (the former is the correct form).
- **Engineering applications:** queueing ($\pi_n$ = fraction of time system has $n$ customers), PageRank (web-graph stationary distribution), machine availability, weather climatology, ALOHA channel idle fraction.
- **Punchline to remember:** *Long-run proportions are not about predicting the future — they describe the time-average behaviour of a stable stochastic process.*
